chapters.summary: Council staff opens the joint Finance and Housing & Buildings Committee hearing, asks attendees to silence electronics and not approach the dais.
chapters.title: Hearing called to order and housekeeping

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Welcome to the Committee on Finance and Housing, joint with, excuse me, welcome to the Committee on Finance, joint with Housing and Building.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this time, we ask that you please silence all electronics.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And at no point, please do not approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chair, you may begin.

chapters.summary: Council Member Linda Lee, Finance Chair, welcomes members and agency partners from HPD and DOF, introduces the hearing topic on financial tools for maintaining Mitchell-Lama housing affordability.
chapters.title: Chair Lee opens oversight hearing on Mitchell-Lama affordability

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: To technically do that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Good afternoon, I'm Councilmember Linda Lee, Chair of the Finance Committee, and welcome to today's hearing, oversight hearing concerning financial tools for maintaining the affordability of Mitchell-Lama housing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm pleased to be joined, I know the speaker's gonna be joining us soon, and my colleague, Chair of the Committee on Housing and Buildings, Councilmember Pierina Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we've also been joined by Councilmember Narcisse, Councilmember Maloney, Councilmember Aldabal, Councilmember Dinowitz, and I believe Councilmember Joseph is on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So welcome also to our partners from the Department of Housing Preservation and Development and the Department of Finance.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you all for joining us today to answer our questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And before I go any further, oh wait, no, we're going to have her do her statements later.

chapters.summary: Lee reviews the Council's prior steps: extending property tax exemptions, expanding benefits for buildings staying in the program, and the FY25 state budget reducing shelter rent tax from 10% to 5%.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on Council actions to preserve Mitchell-Lama housing

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we are here today as the housing affordability crisis continues to challenge New York City.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: as we in city government continue to work to ease the burden of New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The hearing is not intended to litigate the value of the Mitchell-Lama program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This council has long supported and continues to support the feasibility and longevity of the program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As was highlighted during our last oversight hearing on Mitchell-Lama affordability last October,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The City Council has taken multiple steps to preserve Mitchell-Lama housing, extending property tax exemptions for developments that had paid off their mortgages, incentivizing them to remain in the program,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: enacting laws expanding benefits previously unavailable if improvements were financed through government loans or grants to Mitchell-Lama buildings that commit to stay in the program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Further, the fiscal year 2025 New York State budget, with support from Governor Hochul and the state legislature, further reduced the local tax burden on these developments through the New York Shelter Rent Tax Law.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This law caps local and school taxes at 5% of a development's annual shelter rent, down from the previous minimum of 10%.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Some properties may qualify for greater exemptions depending on prior Board of Estimate resolutions.

chapters.summary: Lee emphasizes the need for continued oversight with HPD and DOF to explore existing and potential new tools to support Mitchell-Lama viability without passing costs to tenants.
chapters.title: Chair Lee calls for exploring new financial tools with agencies

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Even with these actions, it is essential that this oversight continues with our agencies and the administration as we look forward to exploring existing and potential new tools

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: to support the long-term viability of Mitchell-Lama developments from our counterparts at the Department of Housing, Preservation and Development, and the Department of Finance, and not passing the burden to tenants in Mitchell-Lama developments.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: developments, rentals, and co-ops.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I now want to turn it over to my co-chair for this hearing, Councilmember and Chair Sanchez, for her opening statement.

chapters.summary: Council Member Pierina Sanchez describes physical and financial deterioration across Mitchell-Lama developments, hazardous conditions, rising rents, and demands HPD come with solutions rather than reactive responses.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez on deteriorating Mitchell-Lama conditions and oversight failures

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair Lee, and good afternoon, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Pierina Sanchez, Chair of the Committee on Housing and Buildings, and I'm pleased to co-chair today's joint oversight hearing with Councilmember Lee, as well as I'm calling him the honorary co-chair, Councilmember Dinowitz, who brought us together.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Regarding Tracy Towers.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: For more than 70 years, the Mitchell-Lama program has provided an essential source of stable, affordable housing for middle and moderate income New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: But today, that promise seems to be in jeopardy.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Too many Mitchell-Lama developments are deteriorating, physically, financially, or both.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Residents are living with hazardous conditions, deteriorating building systems, rising rents and carrying charges, and growing uncertainty about whether they will be able to remain in their homes.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: This is not a minor problem at a handful of isolated buildings.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: It is a deeply troubling trend across a critical affordable housing portfolio.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And it demands more than just descriptions that our buildings are aging, costs are increasing.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We need our agencies, we need HPD to come to the table with solutions that stabilize developments that are already in distress,

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: protect residents from displacement, and fundamentally strengthen the city's oversight so that we prevent the next crisis instead of merely reacting after a development has reached a breaking point.

chapters.summary: Sanchez reviews the program's 1955 creation, 269 developments statewide, 170 in NYC, HPD's supervisory responsibilities including budget approval, rent increases, inspections, and public investment in preservation.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez on Mitchell-Lama program history and HPD oversight role

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Created in 1955, Mitchell

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Chalama encouraged private developers to build rental and cooperative housing by offering low-interest mortgages and property tax exemptions.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: In exchange, owners accepted limits on their profits, resident incomes, rent levels, purchasing prices, and carrying charges.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Since the program began, 269 developments containing more than 105,000 apartments have been built statewide.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: An estimated 170 of those are in New York City, and about 60% of those are supervised by HPD.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: These developments were made possible through public subsidies, and public oversight was intended to preserve their affordability, financial stability, and physical condition.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: For city-supervised developments, HPD approves budgets and rent or carrying charge increases, monitors finances, oversees tenant eligibility and waiting lists, and conducts inspections.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: The city and state have also invested significant public resources in preservation.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: HPD testified just earlier this year that its 10-year capital plan includes $1.1 billion

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: for Michilama housing, some of which was secured through this council through the City of Yes negotiations, while the state's fiscal year 2027 budget, as Shirley mentioned, includes $80 million in new appropriations accessible to developments in the city.

chapters.summary: Sanchez cites 10% of remaining Mitchell-Lamas in financial distress, 37 developments with ~29% average rent increases in 2025, and a March 2026 state audit finding hazardous conditions and $2.3M in misspent funds.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez on financial distress data and state audit findings

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And yet, despite decades of public oversight and substantial public investment, too many Michilama residents are living with both deteriorating conditions and rapidly rising housing costs.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: At least 10% is our understanding of the city's remaining.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Michoamas are experiencing financial distress.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And in 2025 alone, 37 developments received rent increases averaging approximately 29%.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Compare that to this year's 0% increase for rent-stabilized apartments.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: A March 2026 audit by the state controller found hazardous or unsafe conditions, as well as structural issues at 12 of 15 developments examined statewide, including every single one of the 10 New York City developments that were sampled.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: The audit also identified approximately $2.3 million in misspent or inadequately documented funds.

chapters.summary: Sanchez highlights Tracy Towers and Kingsbridge Arms in her district as examples of severe financial and physical distress, with over $1M in debt and inability to cover basic operating expenses.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez on Tracy Towers and Kingsbridge Arms as distress examples

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Those findings should set off alarm bells.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: They demand basic and urgent questions like how can housing that has been continuously supervised by the government fall into such severe financial and physical distress?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Wasn't this oversight intended to prevent precisely this outcome?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And if HPD has been monitoring these developments all along, when did it first see warning signs?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: What did it do in response?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Were inadequate reserves, mounting debt, deferred maintenance, and deteriorating building systems allowed to reach crisis levels?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And most importantly, what is HPD going to do differently moving forward?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: In addition to the situation at Tracy Towers, which we'll talk about at length, I highlight Kingsbridge Arms.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: In my neighborhood, 2865 Kingsbridge Terrace presents another deeply troubling example.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: The development has over $1 million in debt.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Its physical and financial conditions have become so dire that managers have been unable to manage the development.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: actually relies on additional debt to cover even basic operating expenses, including something as simple as trash bags for the garbage room, while major renovations and essential repairs remain unaddressed.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Residents call us, they email our offices, they stop us at community events because they need help.

chapters.summary: Sanchez argues HPD oversight must mean proactive capital planning, adequate reserves, timely repairs, transparent accounting, and early detection of distress before residents reach a breaking point.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez calls for proactive HPD oversight and early intervention

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: In closing, HPD oversight should mean more than simply monitoring a building's decline and then presenting residents with enormous rent or carrying charge increases as the only available solution.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: It must mean proactive capital planning, maintaining adequate reserves, enforcing timely repairs, ensuring responsible management, requiring transparent accounting so that our affordable housing can remain as such in the long term.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: It must mean detecting distress early and intervening decisively before residents and developments reach a breaking point.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We're looking forward to a productive and solutions-focused discussion because Mitchell-Lamas were founded on a public promise that New Yorkers can have safe, stable, and affordable home even if they could not afford the private market.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Our responsibility is not simply to celebrate that promise, but to make certain our city is continuing to keep it.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I look forward to testimony.

chapters.summary: Sanchez thanks her committee and district staff, then returns the floor to Chair Lee, who also thanks staff and swears in witnesses.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez thanks staff and returns to Chair Lee

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And before I close, I want to just thank my team, Ben, Kim, Gerard, Dylan, Maria, Maria Jose, Soraya, and Oriana from our district, and the Housing and Buildings Committee staff,

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Senior Legislative Counsel Austin Malone, Legislative Counsel Billy Eck, Senior Policy Analyst Jose Conde, Policy Analyst Dirk Spencer, and Finance Analyst Carla Naranjo.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And Spencer Kuhn.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: With that, back to Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So before we get started, I'd like to take a quick minute to thank the entire council, finance division, housing and buildings, and land use staff for their efforts in preparing today's hearing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know that you guys did a lot behind the scenes, so I really appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I'll now turn it over to committee counsel to swear on the witnesses for their testimony.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, really quickly.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've also been joined by Councilmember Mealy as well as Councilmember Hudson and Councilmember Riley and Councilmember Ressler.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you swear to tell the truth, not but the truth before this council and the committee members?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Adam Phillips, HPD First Deputy Commissioner, introduces himself and colleagues Alika Amprey-Samuel and Julie Walpert, and begins describing the Mitchell-Lama program's role in NYC's affordable housing stock.
chapters.title: HPD First Deputy Commissioner Phillips introduces agency testimony

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Can you all hear me?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Sanchez and Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I'm Adam Phillips, the first deputy commissioner of HPD, and I'm pleased to be joined by my colleagues, Alika Amprey-Samuel, deputy commissioner of asset management and property management, and Julie Walpert, our assistant commissioner for housing supervision.

chapters.summary: Phillips details approximately 90,000 Mitchell-Lama apartments in 134 NYC developments, split between city (HPD) and state (HCR) supervision, with two-thirds co-ops and one-third rentals, all privately owned and managed.
chapters.title: HPD on Mitchell-Lama portfolio composition and governance

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The Mitchell-Lama program is a critical and unique part of our city's affordable housing stock.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The program was created by the state legislature in 1955 to serve middle class families, and since then it has provided opportunities for hundreds of thousands of New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Today, there are approximately 90,000 Mitchell-Lama apartments in 134 developments spread across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Approximately half of these apartments are supervised by the city through HPD, and the other half are supervised by the state through HCR.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Within HPD's Mitchell-Lama portfolio, two-thirds are co-ops and one-third are rentals.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: All Mitchell Llamas, whether they are co-ops or rentals, are privately owned and managed housing companies that are subject to oversight by HPD or HCR.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: In a Mitchell Lama rental building, a private landlord is the owner and is responsible for maintenance and

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: and long-term capital planning.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Mitchell-Lama co-ops are controlled by their residents, who are shareholders.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Each year, the residents elect a volunteer board of directors that has the responsibility of overseeing operations and long-term capital planning for their co-op on behalf of the shareholders.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The responsibility to serve on a board of directors is great,

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: and we thank all the residents who take on the task.

chapters.summary: Phillips states the portfolio faces rising operating costs, significant capital improvement needs, and uneven rental/maintenance income, positioning these as challenges requiring a path to stabilization.
chapters.title: HPD on Mitchell-Lama portfolio at a crossroads

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We have seen thousands of New Yorkers build their lives in these affordable co-ops and rentals.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Although the program was originally designed to house middle-income families, over time, Mitchell-Lamas came to offer New Yorkers at a range of incomes a chance to find stability in neighborhoods they love, giving them an affordable place to pursue their ambitions,

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: raise their families, and age in place successfully.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We are deeply committed to ensuring the affordability and physical condition of this stock for decades to come.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: While the Mitchell-Lama program has been incredibly successful at providing affordable housing to families across a broad range of incomes, it now sits at a crossroads.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The portfolio faces rising operating costs, significant capital improvement needs, and uneven rental or maintenance fee income streams.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We'll discuss each of these concerns in more detail and provide a path to stabilize the program for the years to come.

chapters.summary: Phillells details skyrocketing insurance premiums, increasing utility costs, and rising staff salaries making it harder for Mitchell-Lamas to balance budgets or fund capital improvements.
chapters.title: HPD on rising operating costs across Mitchell-Lama portfolio

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The Mitchell-Lama portfolio has experienced massive increases in operating costs, as seen across all types of affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Insurance premiums have skyrocketed.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Utility costs are increasing.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Salaries for maintenance and building staff have increased significantly.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: All these rising operating costs

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: have made it more challenging than ever for Mitchell-Lamas to maintain a balanced budget, let alone chart a sustainable path for major capital improvements.

chapters.summary: Phillips notes much of the stock is 50-70 years old, with major systems at end of life, deferred capital, and decarbonization requirements leading to billions in estimated capital needs over the next decade.
chapters.title: HPD on aging housing stock and billions in capital needs

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Much of the Mitchell-Lama housing stock was constructed between 50 to 70 years ago.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: As a result, co-ops and rentals alike have major building systems nearing the end or surpassing their useful lives.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The age of the housing stock, deferred capital improvements, and compliance with decarbonization requirements has led to high estimated capital needs amounting to billions over the next decade.

chapters.summary: Phillips explains Mitchell-Lamas follow a different process than rent-stabilized buildings, with HPD legally required to approve increases to cover costs, and that increases often make up for many years at once.
chapters.title: HPD on erratic rent increase process and statutory requirements

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: In many cases, Mitchell-Lamas experience inconsistent rent or maintenance fee increases.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: This is a result of the unique regulatory structure of the program.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Whereas rent-stabilized buildings follow a framework for rent increases as advised by the Rent Guidelines Board, Mitchell-Lamas follow a completely different process, determining their rent or maintenance fee increases based on the need to keep a balanced budget.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Per state law, HPD is legally required to approve rent and maintenance fee increases up to the amount necessary to cover a building's costs.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Failing to approve an increase could risk violating state law.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It is not HPD's statutory role to determine whether to increase rents.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The responsibility and decision-making around the process is in the hands of the private housing companies.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: When buildings finally do raise their rents or maintenance fees, it can sometimes be making up for many years all at once.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: HPD understands that this practice can be painful for tenants and shareholders, and HPD assists developments with reviewing their income and expenses to maintain a balanced budget.

chapters.summary: Phillips notes 37 developments saw average 29% increases in 2025, comparable to Tracy Towers' 30%, arguing Tracy is not exceptional and reflecting portfolio-wide challenges.
chapters.title: HPD on 2025 rent increases and Tracy Towers in context

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: 37 Mitchell-Lama developments saw rent or maintenance fee increases.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: These saw an average 29% increase over multiple years, comparable to the 30% at Tracy Towers.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: In this context, it becomes clear that Tracy Towers is not entirely exceptional.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The financial challenges facing it are similar to those facing the portfolio as a whole.

chapters.summary: Phillips discusses SCRI/DRI enrollment efforts, the statutory cap on over-income surcharges preventing cross-subsidization, and the need for legislative action to raise the SCRI income cap locally.
chapters.title: HPD on SCRI/DRI enrollment and statutory limits on income-based charges

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We are committed to reducing the number of households impacted by helping them take advantage of SCRI and DRI and advocating for additional rental subsidies from the federal government.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We are also committed to reducing operating costs to minimize the necessary increases across the portfolio.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: In addition to the sometimes erratic frequency of rent or maintenance fee increases, statutorily, Mitchell-Lamas cannot increase rents or maintenance fees for only those who can afford it.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Under the original bill text, higher income households cannot be charged a higher increase than their lower income neighbors.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: This is a common practice used to equitably support the finances of mixed income buildings, but this is not an option available to Mitchell-Lamas.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: While there is a modest surcharge for over-income households, it is capped by statute, meaning that we cannot use higher revenues from those who can afford it to cross-subsidize those who might not.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: These challenges are not insurmountable.

chapters.summary: Phillips highlights the administration's block-by-block housing plan dedicating hundreds of millions across FY27-28 for capital improvements in city-supervised Mitchell-Lamas, and a holistic portfolio-wide approach.
chapters.title: HPD on block-by-block plan capital commitments for Mitchell-Lamas

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Early on, this administration recognized the challenges facing the Mitchell-Lama portfolio, and we took proactive steps to address them.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: In the block-by-block housing plan, the administration laid out a plan to dedicate hundreds of millions of dollars across fiscal years.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: 27 and 28 to specifically target capital improvements in city supervised Mitchell Llamas.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We are also committed to taking a holistic portfolio wide approach to addressing the capital needs and broader challenges facing the city's Mitchell Lama portfolio.

chapters.summary: Phillips urges Council collaboration to enact the statewide SCRI income cap increase from $50K to $75K locally, and discusses HPD's housing supervision team working to contain operating costs.
chapters.title: HPD on SCRI income cap expansion and operating cost containment

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Recognizing the importance of this housing stock and the challenges it faces, we committed to developing a comprehensive set of tools to put the Mitchell-Lama portfolio on a more sustainable path.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: To lessen the impact of rent and maintenance fee increases while ensuring a balanced budget, HPD commits to enrolling eligible households in SCRI and DRI.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Recently, the statewide income cap for households eligible for SCRI increased from $50,000 to $75,000.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: With legislative action from the City Council, we can begin enacting this change on the local level.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: On the expense side, HPD's housing supervision team will continue to work with the private housing companies to contain operating costs.

chapters.summary: Phillips describes Mitchell-Lamas exploring sale of parking lots and undeveloped land to fund trusts offsetting expenses, paying for climate upgrades and capital needs, and creating additional housing.
chapters.title: HPD on exploring land sales and new revenue models for Mitchell-Lamas

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We are also exploring new revenue models to enable capital improvements and reduce, where possible, future increases.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Some Mitchell Llamas are exploring the sale of their parking lots and other undeveloped land where new housing would be built.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: This could provide millions of dollars to fund a trust that would offset increasing expenses, helping to minimize future rent or maintenance fee increases.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It could also pay for climate upgrades and major capital needs such as roof repairs, elevator replacements, and more.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: In the process, it will also create more housing to help address

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: our citywide housing shortage.

chapters.summary: Phillips expresses confidence that with responsible stewardship and new investment, Mitchell-Lamas can serve New Yorkers for decades, and invites Council collaboration.
chapters.title: HPD closes testimony with commitment to long-term Mitchell-Lama preservation

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We believe that with responsible financial stewardship and new investment, the Mitchell-Lama portfolio can serve

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: New Yorkers for decades to come, allowing more families to stay in their neighborhoods while paying affordable rents or maintenance fees, or to experience the stability of homeownership for the first time.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Thank you for sharing our concerns for the physical and financial future of these developments.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: After 70 years, their needs are great, but so is their potential.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We will continue to work collaboratively with you all to protect these developments, maintaining their affordability and their physical upkeep for the next generation.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We're happy to answer any questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Also want to recognize we've been joined by Jay Sanchez as well as Councilmember Brooks Powers on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I'm actually going to first turn it over to Chair Sanchez for her questions.

chapters.summary: Sanchez presses HPD on whether its model is proactive or reactive, questioning why increases are presented as making up for many years at once if financials are reviewed regularly. Phillips explains the process of reviewing financials and planning for capital improvements.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez questions HPD on proactive vs. reactive asset management

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And good afternoon again.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you so much for that context and testimony.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: My first question, I just want to start with...

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Something that you say in your testimony, HPD is legally required to approve rent and maintenance fee increases up to the amount necessary to cover the building's costs, and failing to approve an increase would risk violating the state law.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I highlight this point in your testimony because it doesn't answer a question, a fundamental question that we here at the council have, which is what is HPD's asset management?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: model?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Are you fundamentally being proactive or reactive?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Are you waiting for the buildings to come to you with an application for an increase?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Or are you monitoring as an agency the building's health and the financial health of the building so that you can smooth out over time the increases that are needed at the building?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So our housing supervision team works with the housing companies to regularly review income and expense statements from each housing company and their annual financial audits.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So by reviewing the existing financials, we can work with the...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: co-op boards or the private owners to come up with a plan to address any increases that could be coming up.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So then why is it that if you are regularly as an agency reviewing financials at a building, increases can be presented in a way that is making up for many years at a time?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So in some cases, we are planning for long-term capital improvements.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: More often than not, Mitchell-Lamas won't come to us for the increase until the pre-development process is completed to do the capital work.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So it becomes kind of one fell swoop of addressing any increases to meet operating needs and then support major capital improvements.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Sanchez argues HPD should move toward gradual increases over time and asks how the agency understands its asset management role post-financing. Phillips describes monitoring during and after construction.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez on smoothing rent increases and HPD's asset management responsibility

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I still think that something is being missed here.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: If you are having regular conversations, there should be gradual increases over time.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I think that's a direction the agency should try to move in to smooth things out for tenants and shareholders in these properties.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So stepping back at a programmatic level, how does HPD understand its asset management responsibility after the initial development and preservation of a deal, of the financing of a deal closes?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Is the agency's model designed to prevent financial and physical distress or primarily to intervene once a project has become high risk?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So after a project closes on financing with HPD, during construction, we monitor construction along with the private lender, which is typically HDC.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So during construction, we're working monthly to ensure that requisitions are processed appropriately and that capital needs are being addressed.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And then after the construction period ends, our housing supervision team continues to work with the board or the owner of the housing company.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: To monitor income, expenses, and then future capital needs.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks about the Mayor's Management Report showing a five-year increase in high-risk rental and cooperative indicators. Phillips discusses working with owners on preservation outcomes.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez on five-year increase in high-risk Mitchell-Lama indicators

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: What explains the five-year increase in the mayor's management reports, high-risk rental and cooperative indicators that are showing that we're moving in the wrong direction in terms of the physical stock in these properties?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Sorry, can you repeat the question?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Actually,

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So in the mayor's management report, there's been a five-year increase with respect to HPD's management of high-risk rentals and co-ops.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: What explains that?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Are these Mitchell Llamas specifically or rent-stabilized stock?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: No, it is broader than Mitchell Llamas, but includes Mitchell Llamas.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So for any distressed portfolio or any distressed building, we will work with the owner to ensure that they can put together a loan application and enter our pipeline for a preservation outcome.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Speaker Julie Menin thanks the chairs, references her June 25 letter to the administration about Tracy Towers' 30% rent increase, and frames the hearing as examining whether the city is using all available tools before asking tenants to bear costs.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin delivers opening statement on Mitchell-Lama crisis

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I want to acknowledge that we were joined.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We are joined by Speaker Julie-Menin, and I want to give her the floor.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And really, I want to thank Chair Lee and Chair Sanchez for convening this very important hearing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So I've got an opening statement, and then I'm going to go right into questions and apologize for my tardiness, but we actually have a hearing across the street that I will just say is quite crowded on a very hot topic.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So, first of all, for generations, New York City's Mitchell Lama program has provided, as we all know, affordable and stable homes for working class families, for older adults, and for middle income New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: These residents keep our city literally running.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: They are our teachers, postal workers, our caretakers, police officers.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Allowing them not only to stay here, but to thrive here is absolutely essential to their future and to our city's future as well.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So today's hearing comes at a pivotal moment.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: On June 25th, 2026, I and other elected officials penned a letter to the mayoral administration seeking answers on how the administration plans to address a proposed cumulative 30%

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: rent increase at Tracy Towers.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: This rent increase has brought renewed attention to the financial challenges facing Mitchell Lama developments across the city, rentals and co-ops alike.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The president of the Tenant Association of Tracy Towers warned that if approved, some residents

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: would be forced out of their units.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Tenants'jobs, families, and homes are at stake.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The tenants'lives literally depend on this response.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: While Tracy Towers is, of course, the immediate catalyst for this conversation and for this hearing, the issues before us extend beyond one single development.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: They raise fundamental questions about whether New York City is doing everything within its power to preserve affordability while ensuring the long-term financial health of its much-alama portfolio.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: No one disputes that these developments are facing real financial pressures.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Rising insurance premiums, labor costs, utility expenses, security costs, debt service, and decades of deferred maintenance have placed significant strain on so many buildings.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: At the same time, residents are asking difficult but very reasonable questions.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: If tenants are being asked to absorb rent increase approaching or exceeding 30%,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Why do longstanding maintenance issues persist?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Why are buildings still grappling with aging elevators, deteriorating infrastructure, and other conditions that have gone unaddressed?

chapters.summary: Menin cites the state comptroller audit identifying chronic deterioration and financial oversight weaknesses, arguing preservation requires strong stewardship, transparent management, and strategic public investment.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on state audit findings and need for stronger stewardship

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And are rent increases becoming the default solution before every available financial and preservation tool has first been exhausted?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Those questions have become even more pressing following the recent state controller audit, which identified chronic physical deterioration at many Mitchell Lama developments, as well as weaknesses in financial oversight.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: site and accountability.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The findings truly underscore that preserving these buildings requires more than just balancing operating budgets.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: It requires strong stewardship, transparent management, and strategic public investment as well.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: This hearing is not about questioning whether Mitchell Lama developments need financial support.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: They do.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: It's about examining how that support is delivered and whether the city is making full use of the tools at its disposal before asking these tenants to bear the cost.

chapters.summary: Menin frames the hearing as balancing two responsibilities: preserving Mitchell-Lama financial viability while preserving the affordability that defines the program, and transitions to questioning.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin on balancing financial viability with affordability

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We should be asking whether preservation financing, capital investments, operating subsidies, insurance reforms, refinancing opportunities, and stronger oversight of management practices can reduce the need for significant rent increases.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We should also examine whether HPD's oversight framework provides sufficient transparency and accountability,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: whether residents have meaningful opportunities to participate in decisions that are affecting their homes and their livelihoods, and whether existing affordability protections, including proactive SCRI enrollment required under local

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: law 44 of 2024, are being fully implemented.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Ultimately, this hearing is about balancing two responsibilities that are not in conflict, preserving the financial viability of Mitchell Lama developments while preserving the affordability that defines the very program itself.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: These goals must go hand in hand.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I look forward to today's hearing and working collaboratively.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: to identify policies which ensure that balance is being maintained.

chapters.summary: Menin asks for a comprehensive list of city-run Mitchell-Lama developments and rent increases over 10 years. Phillips says they can provide it later; Menin insists it should be discussed on the record now.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin presses HPD for rent increase data requested in June 25 letter

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So I'm just going to go jump right into my questions.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We sent a letter on June 25th.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And so I'd like to just get an update on some of the questions that we asked in that letter.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: First and foremost, to provide a comprehensive list of city-run Mitchell Lama developments and the rent increases they have incurred over the past 10 years and the proposed upcoming rent increases.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So I think it would be easiest for us to supply that information after the hearing.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It's a very long list with a long schedule of increases.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: No, no, that's the purpose of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The purpose of the hearing is to go on the record and to talk about which buildings are getting a rent increase.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I mean, look, we know about Tracy Towers, but we're hearing from Mitchell Lama residents throughout the city who are saying they're getting a rent increase.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So that's the whole purpose of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So we want that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Being discussed now.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: If you can just give us a second to go through our files.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Sure, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we'll work on, as the hearing's going on, if you don't mind, we can work on putting the list together and then report back later in the hearing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I mean, I do just want to say we never received a response to our letter, and this hearing is upcoming.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: We are not asking some surprise question.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: This is literally something we asked on June 25th, and that you all should be prepared to be providing to us.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Understood.

chapters.summary: Menin asks whether HPD has conducted a comprehensive financial review of all city-supervised Mitchell-Lamas and which developments face the most significant upcoming increases. Phillips says they regularly review financials but doesn't have specifics at hand.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin asks HPD about comprehensive financial review of all Mitchell-Lamas

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, I'm going to ask the second question then.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Has a comprehensive review of the financial condition of all city-supervised Mitchell-Lama developments, including which developments are currently experiencing

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: financial stress or are projected to require the most significant rent increases within the next five years, been conducted?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: These are all from the letter.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So, again, these are not new questions.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: These are ones that literally we've asked you all weeks ago, and we've received no response.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we regularly work with the Mitchell Lama housing companies to review income and expenses, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we regularly get income and expenses and audited financials and work with the companies to ensure that we can minimize rent or maintenance fee increases.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So has there been a conference?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Comprehensive review of the financial condition of all of the city-supervised Michelama developments?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We're regularly reviewing the financials of all city-supervised Michelamas.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And, okay, can you share the results with these two committees?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: For every single Mitchell Lama that's city supervised?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, that's really what the, that's the point of the hearing is to look at the financial oversight.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I mean, I don't have that in front of me right now, unfortunately.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: That is basic information.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: I mean, we asked about this in the– I mean, I'm just surprised we sent this letter that the administration is not prepared to answer the questions that we asked about three weeks ago.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, unfortunately, I don't have that at my fingertips at the moment.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So we would like to get that, and we want that in the coming days.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So that's a confirmation that these two committees will get that information?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We will work our best to supply you with that information.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Menin asks about HPD's oversight mechanisms to ensure management companies fulfill fiduciary responsibilities. Phillips describes working with private housing companies and overseeing property managers.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin questions HPD on oversight and accountability mechanisms

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: The fourth question we asked in the letter was,

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: to describe the oversight and accountability mechanisms that HPD utilizes to ensure management companies fulfill their fiduciary responsibilities?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we work with the private housing companies.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The private housing companies, it's their responsibility to hire a property manager, and then we oversee the private owners and the co-op boards to ensure that the property

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: managers are fulfilling their requirements per the property management agreement.

chapters.summary: Menin asks what additional resources HPD plans to deploy to prevent similar increases. Phillips discusses SCRI income cap expansion, advocating for additional Section 8, and federal resources.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin asks HPD about tools to prevent Tracy Towers-style increases

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: What are the additional resources and strategies that HPD plans to deploy to prevent rent increases similar to those that are occurring at Tracy Towers?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we have screen jury already at our disposal, and we would request collaboration with the city council to, on the local level, increase the income cap from $50,000 to $75,000.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So on the state level, that was legislated, but we still have to do something locally to ensure that the cap goes from $50,000 to $75,000.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We also would appreciate collaboration with the council to advocate for additional federal sources.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The biggest operating subsidy outside of screen jury that we could all advocate for is additional Section 8.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: That is a federal source that has been neglected, to put lightly, for quite some time.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And we're working as best as we can to advocate for additional Section 8 resources to help mitigate any maintenance or...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: rent increase.

chapters.summary: Menin asks how many developments have closed under the multifamily housing rehabilitation loan program and whether Tracy Towers has applied. Phillips reports 12 projects in the pipeline and Tracy Towers' two-phase closing timeline.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin asks about HPD loan programs and Tracy Towers financing

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And how many city supervised developments have closed under the multifamily housing rehabilitation loan program or the Michelama reinvestment program?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Just one second.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We have that.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So currently we have 12 projects in our pipeline.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We're looking for the number.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I believe we have it with us for total projects that have ever closed on an HRP loan.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And what developments have applied for this?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And has Tracy Towers applied for it?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Sorry, can you repeat that?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Well, which developments?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: You mentioned 12.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Which developments?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And then is Tracy Towers one of them?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So Tracy Towers is a two-phase closing.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we close on financing at the end of June 2026.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And then we expect by the end of calendar year 2027 to close on the second phase of financing.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And, sorry, just to jump back, we do have...

chapters.summary: HPD staff read into the record a list of developments with pending or scheduled rent increases, including Washington Square Southeast, Sam Burt, Trinity House, First Atlantic Terminal, Jefferson Towers, Tracy Towers, Masrick Towers, Rupert House, and Lincoln-Amsterdam, with requested increase percentages.
chapters.title: HPD reads list of Mitchell-Lama developments with pending rent increases

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: the list of projects with the rent increases pending.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Will you just read them into the record?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: How many are there?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: It's a large list.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Let me read it and then we can.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And they're at different stages of the.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So these are all the Mitchell Lama, just to be clear, I want to make, these are all the Mitchell Lama developments across the city that are going to face rent increases, correct?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So, well, they're in different stages of the increase process.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And can you specify the amount of the increase at the same time?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So I can specify the amount that they have requested, but we haven't done any of the reviews yet for the list that I'm about to name.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: First, and I think, I'm not sure what the order is, so I'm just going to go through what they sent me.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Washington Square Southeast is a co-op.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And they're looking at a three-stage increase of 15, 10, and 5.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: The next one is Sam Burt, which is also a co-op.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And with both of those, those were just the applications were received.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And that increase that they requested is 15, 7, and 7.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And again, this is the co-op.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: requesting this.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: The next is Trinity House, which is a rental where the public hearing is scheduled.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And that they're looking at three stages of seven, seven and seven.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: The next is First Atlantic Terminal, which is a co-op, and they are looking at 2.5% and 2.5%.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Next is Jefferson Towers, and they are looking at a 10 and a 6, and the hearing is scheduled for there.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Tracy Towers, we recently held the hearing.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Masrick Towers is...

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Looking to increase their to 15 and 5.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And Luna, I'm sorry, Rupert House, the hearing was completed.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And they're looking at a 15, 10, and 5.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Lincoln, Amsterdam, the hearing was completed, and they're looking at a 30 and a 10, but I just want to actually note on that one, they haven't had an increase in 25 years.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And Jefferson also has not had an increase in many years.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: They both have commercial space that supports the developments.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And those are the, that's what we have.

chapters.summary: Menin asks how HPD will help tenants across these developments. Phillips describes targeted SCRI outreach, and Julie Walpert provides statistics on 2025 increases: 37 developments, average 26% over multiple years, 25 with 20%+ increases.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin asks how HPD will help tenants facing significant increases

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, and how is HPD going to help these tenants who are all going to be facing very significant rent increases?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, so we do targeted outreach to enroll households that are eligible on to SCRI.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And then I'll let my colleague, Alika, just talk a little bit about what our process is to do outreach to the households that are eligible for SCRI.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: Good afternoon, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: I just wanted to just give you some highlighted, so it's like high level numbers related to the question around

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: the past 10 years and the proposed rent increases, because we'll be able to, we can give you these numbers and then follow up with what these numbers actually mean.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: But I just wanted to get this on the record.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: We've had 37 Mitchell Lama developments that saw rent and carrying charge increases in 2025.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: So 37 Mitchell Lama developments saw increases in 2025.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: These saw an average of 26% increase over multiple years.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: And that was comparable to what Adam, the first deputy commissioner, was saying with the 28% at Tracy Towers.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: But 15 were initiated in 2025, and the rest were initiated between 2022 and 2024.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: 25 of those involved a 20% or greater increase over several years.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: And seven of those involved a 20% or greater increase in just 2025.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: Five Michelama developments have filed for and received rate increases so far this year in 2026.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: And the reason why I mentioned these numbers is because we have like the pattern and the trend and we can provide.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: you on an individual basis working directly with the members on the developments within their districts that have asked for the increases and what's going on because that's the work that our

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: portfolio analysts actually do with the managing agents and with the shareholders.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: They go through these numbers on a regular basis, and we can provide you with that information because our portfolio analysts are the asset managers in our housing supervision department.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: And so they have this information.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: This is nothing that is all of a sudden happening.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: It's, we've seen, you already stated it, we've seen increases recently that are drastic, but our portfolio analysts are in these buildings every single day.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: And so we have the information for you and we can sit with each member.

chapters.summary: Menin asks whether HPD requires owners to demonstrate they've exhausted all HPD/HDC refinancing options before approving increases. Phillips confirms HPD takes a holistic view of expenses, income, and existing debt.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin asks if HPD requires owners to exhaust refinancing before increases

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So does HPD require an owner to demonstrate that they have exhausted all HPD HDC refinancing options before HPD approves a rent or carrying charge increase to fund repairs?

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: And if not, why is that not a precondition?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: No, we absolutely do that.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: When a rent increase comes in, we take a broader view of what is happening at the development.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We're analyzing the expenses.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We're analyzing the income.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We're taking a look at the existing debt and how we can refinance or restructure some of that existing debt.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So when the...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The increase comes in.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We're not just looking at only the expenses.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We're looking holistically at the entire project to figure out what is going to be the best stabilization outcome for the development.

chapters.summary: Menin asks what financial tools HPD explored before the Tracy Towers increase. Phillips explains existing and new HPD capital, that residents don't pay for HPD debt service, and the shelter rent tax reduction helped.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin asks about financial tools explored for Tracy Towers

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So for Tracy Towers specifically, what financial tools did HPD explore before determining this proposed approximately 30% rent increase?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So there's existing debt on the project, and there's new HPD capital that went into the project, and we'll go in in two tranches.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And the increase that the residents are experiencing is not going to pay for any of the debt service that's related to the city capital that HPD is putting into the project.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So by ensuring that our debt isn't being paid at all by the residents limits the amount of the increase.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Additionally, the shelter rent tax was recently lowered from 10% to 5%, which I think we all know,

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: which was really helpful to ensure that the increase wasn't as large as it would have been maybe a couple years ago without the lower property tax amounts.

chapters.summary: Menin asks whether Mitchell-Lamas will be eligible for the $100M city-backed insurance program in the FY27 budget. Phillips confirms yes, and hopes it will lower premiums market-wide. Menin notes the Council's pending insurance accountability bill.
chapters.title: Speaker Menin asks about Mitchell-Lama eligibility for city-backed insurance program

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, one last question before I pass it over to my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So in the fiscal 2027 executive budget, $100 million was added to support a city-backed insurance program.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Will Mitchell-Lamas be eligible for the city-backed insurance program?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And more broadly, we're hoping that the insurance plan will not only benefit the units.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: or the owners that go into the program, we're hoping that it'll move the needle on the entire market and lower premiums for everybody.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: That is the intent, is to lower insurance premiums for any project, not just the ones that enroll into the program.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Yeah, and I mean, I would note we are about to pass in the city council our insurance accountability bill, which sets up a new first-in-the-nation insurance accountability office to lower premiums.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: So we look forward to working with the administration on that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Okay, let me pass it back over to...

chapters.summary: Phillips reads a list of Mitchell-Lama developments that have closed on HPD's multifamily housing rehabilitation loan program, including Riverbend, Ryerson Towers, Atlantic, Crown Gardens, Kings Bay, and others.
chapters.title: HPD reads list of developments that closed on HRP loans since 2021

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I do have a list of the Mitchell Llamas that closed on the HRP loans, the housing.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So if you don't mind, I can read this off.

utterances.speaker: Julie Menin
utterances.text: Yeah, if you could read that in.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Riverbend, Ryerson Towers, Village East.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Atlantic, second Atlantic terminal, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Crown Gardens.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Kings Bay 1.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: What is this saying?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Chatterton.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Brighton House, Dennis Lane, High Lawn Terrace, Arvern Preservation.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Oh, there's, sorry, the chicken scratch was a little hard to read.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The Bronxwood Tower, Mazarik Towers, and Village East.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And sorry, one more.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Cedar Manual Mutual Housing Corp.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, I'm going to pass it over to Chair Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Just could you clarify the list you were reading out exactly what applies to those?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Oh, so these are all projects that have closed on HPD's multifamily housing rehabilitation loan program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, I just want to quickly.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Since 2021.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've been joined by my council members, Aviles, Banks, Murano, Feliz, Ferrejas, Majority Leader Abreu.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, go ahead and cheers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, and, hmm?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, Council Member Wilson.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, there we go.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Wow, we have quorum.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We can do stated now, Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks how often Mitchell-Lamas conduct physical needs assessments. Phillips explains the integrated physical needs assessment (IPNA) requirement for projects entering HPD's pipeline, and Julie Walpert notes 48 IPNAs completed since 2020.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez questions HPD on physical needs assessments for Mitchell-Lamas

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So I'm just going to ask a couple of questions, and then because we have so many colleagues here, I just want to move things along, and the chair will ask her questions, and we'll go to colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So just getting a sense of the physical conditions of Micho-Lama properties across the city, can you give us a sense of how often Micho-Lamas are conducting a physical needs assessment?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Per property?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, so any project that's coming to HPD or HDC to get financing is required to complete what's called an integrated physical needs assessment.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So the IP&A is used to assess the outstanding capital needs, short-term, medium-term, long-term, and it's also an energy audit and provides recommendations to do energy efficiency upgrades.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So every project that comes through our pipeline is required to come with an IPNA.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We are constantly seeing Mitchell Llamas come to us for an IPNA or come to us with an IPNA to apply for city capital.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So of the 134...

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Properties in the city, how many of those have come to the agency in the last five years or whatever timeline you have the answer for?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we have 90 developments in our portfolio.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We don't have the exact number that have gone through, that have a current IPNA, but all of the projects that I listed previously that received a loan through HRP, they all had IPNAs.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And any project that's closed on financing with us recently would have completed an IPNA.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So Mitchell Llamas are not proactively conducting IPNAs.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: They are conducting them when they are coming to HPD for financing.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I would say some are and some are not.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It depends on the individual housing company.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It depends on the individual co-op.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Some are more proactive with diagnosing capital needs, while others wait until the last minute.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And this is not unique to Mitchell Llamas.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We see this with the...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Privately owned rent stabilized stock as well.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And high school students that leave their homework for the last minute.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, exactly.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: How does HPD...

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: I just wanted to add, I'm not sure if...

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: if we were talking about this number, but among the HPD supervised Mitchell Llamas, we've had 48 that have had IPNAs performed since 2020,

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: and another six are currently in the process of obtaining NYSERDA-funded IPNA, which HPD helped to facilitate.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks for a roll-up of capital needs costs from the 48 IPNAs and how HPD monitors whether rent increases translate into improved conditions. Phillips describes monthly construction monitoring and HPD's oversight role.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez on capital need costs and monitoring building conditions post-increase

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: The rest of them are?

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: Six are currently in the process, and we help them to facilitate that conversation with NYSERDA.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So 48 IPNAs completed since 2020.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Do you have a roll-up number of the cost of the capital need in these 48 developments?

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: We can get that to you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.
