utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And then last question before going back to Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: How does HPD monitor whether rent and carrying charge increases or the work that you finance is translating into improvements in building conditions?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So the capital work is overseen by the private lender and by HPD.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So monthly, when construction's happening, they have to requisition for construction loan funds, and the architect, the contractor, and a representative from the bank have to sign off that work was adequately done, and then the funds are released to pay for that work.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So every month during construction, we have folks going out to ensure that the work is being done per the scope of work and drawings.

chapters.summary: Sanchez requests a breakdown of HPD violations within the Mitchell-Lama portfolio. Phillips says they can provide that information.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez asks for HPD violations breakdown in Mitchell-Lama portfolio

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I'm sorry, I fibbed one related last question is, do you have a breakdown of HPD violations?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: within the Michilama portfolio?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Not offhand, but we can provide that.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: That would be helpful.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Shirley.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, we've also been joined by Council Members Brewer and Epstein.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the 8,000 estimated eligible households for SCRI/DRI, how many are enrolled, and what outreach is conducted. Phillips reports 5,500 on Section 8 and 6,000 on SCRI, and Julie Walpert describes constituent service coordinators' outreach.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions HPD on SCRI/DRI enrollment and outreach

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And really quickly, just wanted to go into scree and dree.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I know that Department of Finance manages the main rent freeze programs and is part of their work.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: They use their data to estimate how many households are eligible for scree dree.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And their most recent report from 2025 estimates that there's about 8,000 households in Mitchell-Lama developments that are eligible.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So out of those 8,000, how many of the housing units are currently enrolled in scree-dree rent freeze programs?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So SCRI is administered in Mitchell-Lamas by HPD, by our housing supervision team.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And I think the data point of 8,000 or so households is probably just looking at purely income, and it's not looking at whether or not they have an existing rental subsidy.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So if you have a housing choice voucher, which we call Section 8, you're not eligible for SCRI and vice versa.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: You can only have one rental subsidy program.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We have in our Mitchell Lama portfolio on Section 8, we have 5,500 vouchers, and we have about 6,000 households who are on SCRI.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what is the outreach like for it?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is there any additional targeting?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: outreach to residents that are subject to significant rent increases?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And if so, was such outreach conducted for residents of Tracy Towers as well?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, I think I was alluding earlier that my colleague, Lika, will answer.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Now's your time.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: So within our housing supervision department, we have portfolio analysts who work with the managing agents and the shareholders related to the finances.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: But we also have a team of constituent service coordinators.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: And those are the individuals who go into the building and work directly with the tenants.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: They're doing outreach and engagement.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: Thank you to some of the council members who work with our constituent services team to do workshops.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: Some of the council members have done workshops in their offices to assist.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: And so that team do the direct engagement.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: And it's almost like case management services where individuals who are not able to complete the applications or have the information, they hold their hands and go through every single detail and then also look for as benefit specialists.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: What else are the tenants eligible for?

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: Even down to looking, working with HRA to see if there are any rental arrears.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: And so it's a whole process conducted by our constituent services coordinators.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And of course, the pre-filled screen applications, Councilmember Dinnowitz, that's really, really helpful.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We want to reduce the burden on the tenant to ensure that they can stay stably housed, and that was a big help.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then.

chapters.summary: Lee asks whether HPD partners with nonprofits and how many languages are offered for SCRI outreach. Walpert says they use a language line and seek community partnerships. Lee encourages culturally competent nonprofit partnerships.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on nonprofit partnerships and language access for SCRI outreach

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Tenants associations, also nonprofits, do you partner with any of those?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because when I was on the nonprofit side for many years, I know that we helped a lot of our residents and constituents and, you know, do the casework like you're saying.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So are there partnerships like that?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And also how many different languages are you assisting them in?

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: We are always looking for partners.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: This is a collaborative effort.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: And so, yes, we do.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: And as far as languages, we actually have a language line that assists us with our...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The city language line?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, because sometimes that's questionable, I'm not going to lie.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But yes, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I mean, I will admit it is better to have some sort of assistance than none.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But I would really encourage, especially there's a lot of culturally competent language proficient nonprofits out there as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I know that they are doing this work on the ground.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so it'd be great to see the partnerships there.

chapters.summary: Lee asks the primary reason eligible households don't enroll. Walpert says reasons vary widely, from income issues to household composition, and emphasizes continuous engagement.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on reasons eligible households don't enroll in SCRI

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what's the main eligible, what's the primary reason that eligible households, sorry, do not enroll?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: From what you're sort of hearing back when you do the outreach?

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: There are just, it just runs the gamut.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: It could be anything from they may not enroll because of their income, other people in the household.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: And what we've seen also is just continuous engagement, reaching out to people, ensuring them that they should complete the forms.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: Just so many different issues.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about coordination with DOF. Phillips says HPD fully administers SCRI for Mitchell-Lamas with minimal DOF overlap, though Walpert notes DRI applications are completed by DOF and they coordinate workshops.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on HPD coordination with Department of Finance for SCRI/DRI

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, which is why the pre-filled forms are hopefully going to help.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then also just considering, because I know that sometimes agencies work in silos and not, with all best intentions, I know they try to coordinate and work together, but how has that been with partnering with Department of Finance?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because I know that there probably needs to be a lot of coordination there.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So how has that looked?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we fully enroll and run the SCRI program for Mitchell Llamas.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So the overlap with us and DOF is very minimal and not really necessary.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: But the DRI applications are completed by Department of Finance, but when we go out to do a workshop, we'll bring DRI applications along with us so that we do coordinate with them.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how many Mitchell-Lama developments will reach the 5% shelter rent tax cap and how many will have lower liability. Phillips confirms all have moved to 5% and mentions J51 reauthorization as another tool.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on shelter rent tax cap and development-level tax liability

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then in terms of the tax exemption and tax liability, in 2025, the state imposed a cap, as we heard before.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the cap for each development is either 5% if it's shelter, rent, or carrying charges.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Presumably, some developments will not reach the cap because the value of their tax exemption and title.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: relieves them to a lower tax liability.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So how many Mitchell Lama developments in the city will reach the cap this year and how many will have a local tax liability less than the cap?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So every Mitchell Lama development has already moved from the 10% shelter rent tax down to the 5% shelter rent tax.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Also excited for J51 to be reauthorized because that is another tool in our toolbox if there's capital work happening to be able to lower their property tax liability.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So if we can layer in the lower 5% shelter rent tax with J51 as we're doing capital work, that's another tool that we could use to minimize the increases.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about delinquencies on shelter rent tax and water bills. A DOF representative reports approximately $47.5 million in arrears for property tax and charges. Lee asks about HPD engagement with DEP on water costs.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on Mitchell-Lama delinquencies and total arrears

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how many Mitchell Lama developments are delinquent by more than a quarter on their shelter rent tax, water bills?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What's the total arrears?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think we just checked this.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's about $47.5 million in arrears for property tax and charges that DOF administers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I don't have the number for water because we don't bill those.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And has HPD engaged with DEP on solutions for resolving, because I know that those costs have also increased.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So has there been any engagement in terms of how to bring relief for that as well?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We're looking at options internally to figure out a way to lower water costs for Mitchell-Lamas.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how HPD monitors delinquencies and what happens when a property is delinquent. Phillips describes reviewing financials, engaging housing companies on balanced budgets, and using it as a jumping-off point for capital work.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on HPD monitoring of delinquent properties

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And considering the delinquencies are a red flag for the financial distress, how does HPD monitor these delinquencies and what do you do when you see the property is delinquent?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Delinquent for municipal property taxes?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So when we review the income and expense statements and the audits, we'll immediately see if there's property tax arrears or any other kind of municipal arrears.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And that's, we'll continue to engage with the housing company to ensure that they're going to put together a balanced budget and then start talking about what the increase would look like to satisfy the arrears.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And we also use that as a jumping off point to figure out what kind of capital work can we address at the same time.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks whether any building will be charged less than 5% of shelter rent. Phillips confirms every Mitchell-Lama is now paying 5% shelter rent tax. Lee asks about comparing exemption values against the cap.
chapters.title: Chair Lee clarifies shelter rent tax cap application

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, and just to follow up to the previous one.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Question.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So are you saying that no building will be charged less than 5% of the shelter rent or carrying charge?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Every, yeah, can you phrase that again?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So are you saying that no building will be charged less than 5% of their shelter rent?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Or did you mean the carrying charge?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The shelter rent.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So every Mitchell Lama is now paying a 5% shelter rent tax.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And have you compared the value of each development's exemption against the cap to determine which is lower?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I don't have that information on us, but I think the larger benefit is the 5% of the shelter rent tax.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about Local Law 97 penalty exemptions for Mitchell-Lamas. Phillips says they're exempt through 2035. Lee asks about post-2035 increases; Phillips says they'll provide that information.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on Local Law 97 exemptions for Mitchell-Lamas

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Also, just a side question, because this is a bill that we are looking at as well, but for Mitchell-Lamas, are you exempt, or are there any exemptions for Local Law 97 in terms of the future penalties that are coming up?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because I know those charges are going to be quite a bit.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, so they're exempt through 2035, or until 2035.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then after 2035, because I know that it's increments of the increases, how much is it going to be increased to?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is it at a lower percentage than some of the other co-ops and resident?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I don't have that off the top of my head, but we can supply that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Only because I know with insurance costs and everything else going up, that could be a potential cost in the near future that would be.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: big, but hopefully the J51s and everything else will help as well.

chapters.summary: Lee asks whether HPD provides expense funding for non-capital repairs or operating support. Phillips says no, HPD only provides city capital for capital-eligible rehab items, and advocates for more Section 8.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on HPD capital and expense funding for Mitchell-Lamas

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and then just last couple questions on the capital funding.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you provide expense funding to Mitchell Llamas either for non-capitally eligible repairs or for general operating support?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: No, we don't provide tax levy or expense funds for Mitchell Lamas.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We only provide, like any other affordable housing project, we can only provide city capital to pay for capital eligible rehab items.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Again, the biggest benefit or one of the largest benefits that we could get is additional Section 8 to mitigate.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: the rent increases, which I think we all know is an uphill battle.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes, definitely.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about changes to capital spending since the preliminary budget and how much has been utilized for Mitchell-Lama preservation in FY25-26. Phillips reports the total preservation budget for FY27 is $1.32 billion.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on HPD capital spending changes since preliminary budget

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And during the FY27 preliminary budget hearing, HPD had testified that the agency has, as the Speaker mentioned, spent $1.1 billion for Mitchell Llamas over the past 10 years.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And has the amount of capital spending changed since the preliminary budget?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how much capital funding has been utilized for Mitchell Lama preservation in FY25 and 26?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So the total preservation budget for fiscal year 27 is 1.2, excuse me, $1.32 billion.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And of that, Mitchell Lama Developments will benefit and will receive some of that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, do you know how much?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, so we're expected to preserve in fiscal years 27 to 28 about 2,800 units plus or minus.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It'll depend on the overall capital needs and the overall construction costs as we get closer to closing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about capital commitment rate changes over 10 years and how much of the five-year capital plan is budgeted for Mitchell-Lama preservation. Phillips says commitments have increased and they don't break out Mitchell-Lama separately.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on capital commitment rates and five-year plan for Mitchell-Lamas

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Have the capital commitment rates changed significantly over the past 10 years?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Like the amount that we're lending to Mitchell Llamas?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, I think it's gone up over the past 10 years.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We've been committing more city capital than we previously had.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And in the housing plan, you know, there's a lot, there was specifically hundreds of millions set aside and called out just for Mitchell Llamas, which I...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Pretty sure has not happened in the past.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And also moving to the exec plan for FY27, we saw a total capital commitment of $4.9 billion for preservation for the five-year capital plan, as well as a $500 million total increase in FY31.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So how much of the five-year capital commitment plan is budgeted for Mitchell-Lama preservation?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we don't break out specifically for Mitchell Llamas versus other rent-stabilized stock that's coming through our preservation pipelines.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: But we have more capital than we have in the past, and we know that the needs are pretty significant in Mitchell Llamas.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And as they work through our pipeline and they're prepared for construction loan closing, we'll deploy capital as needed.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks whether the percentage of Mitchell-Lama units is similar year over year. Phillips says they've seen increases in Mitchell-Lamas coming to HPD, driven by aging stock and more forceful HPD encouragement to pursue preservation.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on trends in Mitchell-Lama pipeline and capital deployment

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then just in terms of how you're determining how many units, since you don't separate it out, are you seeing year over year, though, that percentage-wise it's pretty similar year over year?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Or have you seen significant increases, decreases, and what has caused that?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I think we've seen increases in the amount of Mitchell llamas that are coming to us.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I think there's probably two reasons.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I think one is just the age of the housing stock.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Some folks, and it's not unique to Mitchell llamas, can put their heads in the sand and hope that they don't have to do their capital needs and then they wake up and it's time.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I think in other cases, when our housing supervision team is working with the Mitchell Llamas,

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: You know, we want to make sure we're addressing capital needs, and I think we've been a little bit more forceful with saying it's time to– we really need to do a preservation outcome here.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I just want to recognize we've also been joined by Councilmember Wong and anyone else that I missed.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz thanks HPD for visiting Tracy Towers, asks whether HPD is in compliance with Local Law 44 pre-filled SCRI applications, and disputes HPD's claim of full compliance given his office has had to assist many applicants.
chapters.title: Council Member Dinowitz questions HPD on SCRI compliance and Tracy Towers

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so we're going to move into questions of our council members and colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so I want to start with Chair Dynowitz, followed by Councilmember Hudson.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You're de facto chair.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, actual Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I want to thank Chair Sanchez and Speaker Menon for their support in this, not just for the residents of Tracy Towers, some of whom are here, but for residents of Michelama throughout the city.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And thank you for signing the letter.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: This is really critical.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I want to thank you, HPD, for coming to Tracy Towers.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I think you know Michelamas were designed for working class and middle class families.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And you saw and you know the promise of Michelama.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And you saw firsthand what happens when we disinvest from our Michelama housing stock, that the residents of Tracy and residents throughout the city are not only being hit with a 31% rent increase, but living with rats, living with mold,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: living with leaky roofs, living with unrepaired elevators, even though the money was put in for all that.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I think the amount of time we're spending on SCRI is a little ridiculous.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It is a band-aid to the structural problems.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Nevertheless, it is a solution to some of them.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So I do want to ask quickly, are you in compliance with Local Law 44 of 24, my bill, to provide pre-filled SCRI applications?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: To residents of city-run Michelama housing?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yes, we're fully in compliance.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I have had numerous people come to my office eligible for SCRI, and we have had to assist them in filling out these SCRI applications.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It is my belief that you are not fully in compliance, given that we've had to fill out so many applications.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Can you tell me how many applications, pre-filled applications, have you sent out to residents of Michilamas?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So Julie just mentioned a good point.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The increase hasn't gone into effect yet.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So the applications aren't pre-filled until the increase actually happens.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So no increases have happened at Tracy Towers yet.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: They're planned.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There were still people at Tracy, and I think last year, the previous commissioner testified he was fully in compliance.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Nevertheless, still this year, we are filling out SCRI applications.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So it should have been the case even last year that they would have filled out those applications because you would have received their income affidavit at that point.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks whether HPD has ever rejected or modified a proposed rent increase, or if it serves as a rubber stamp. Phillips says HPD has modified increases by finding ways to lower expenses.
chapters.title: Council Member Dinowitz asks if HPD has ever rejected or modified a rent increase

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I want to move on from that because we have, you can see tons of people here, all of whom their Mitchell alarm is facing upwards of 20%, 30% rent increases.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Phillips, you testified HPD is legally required to approve rent and maintenance increases up to the amount necessary.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I want to understand this a bit.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Has there ever been a time that you have rejected a proposed rent increase from someone seeking a rent increase?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I don't have that.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: that knowledge off the top of my head, but to ensure that the building is operating appropriately, we want to make sure that the income can cover the expenses.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I understand what the purpose of the rent increase is, which again, 31% is outlandish.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I do have to say, for the residents of Michilamas, for the residents of Tracy, Cannon, Kingsbridge Arms, all in my...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Some in my district or near my district, you know, 31%, 40%, 50%, I think anywhere is a big increase.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But it is not only big, it is in the face of an administration who's made affordability a centerpiece of their campaign.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And who campaigned on 0% rent increases.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So it isn't just about saying the rent increases too much.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It is about what it feels unfair to see that disparity.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But my question still is, has HPD ever rejected or modified a proposed rent increase, or has it served as a rubber stamp for the company seeking the rent increase?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: No, we've modified in the past.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And the way we'd be able to modify an increase is if we can find a way to lower expenses, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So when the rent increase comes in, it has to cover operating expenses.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We know operating expenses are wildly out of control.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz highlights Tracy Towers' security costs jumping from $1.8M to $2.8M, a nearly 50% increase, and asks whether HPD reviewed financials before proposing the 31% rent increase. Phillips says the increase hasn't gone into effect and they're reviewing expenses further.
chapters.title: Council Member Dinowitz questions 50% security cost increase at Tracy Towers

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I want to talk about one of those operating expenses.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Because for Tracy Towers and their financials for fiscal year 25, security.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Costs total about $1.8 million in the fiscal year ending 25.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: In FY27, they jumped to $2.8 million.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That's nearly a 50% increase in that two-year period.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That's $267 a month per unit in security costs.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Now, as you say you go over these financials, does that seem like a reasonable amount for security costs to increase?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I mean, that's quite a large increase.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I'm not going to say it's not.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We are bringing in the property management firm to meet with HPD to go into even more detail than we already have in the past to get a better understanding.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But I'm sorry, but you're doing this after the rent increase was already proposed.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So help me understand what work HPD does before they even propose an outlandish 31% rent increase on these hardworking tenants.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And we have one example here of a 50% increase in security costs alone.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Help me understand what work you're doing beforehand.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So the rent increase has not gotten to effect yet.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It has not gone into effect.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We do our analysis.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But it's been proposed and you have.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It's been proposed.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It's been proposed and it's a requirement so we can close on the capital financing to start addressing some of the urgent physical conditions.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Now that we've closed on financing.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The increase has not gone into effect yet.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We still have a chance to work with the property management firm to take an even deeper look at the operating costs.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We're not stopping just because the increase

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Has been approved.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz criticizes the 4 PM hearing time for Tracy Towers as inaccessible to working residents, and presses Phillips on whether HPD reviewed financials before the public hearing. Phillips confirms ongoing review.
chapters.title: Council Member Dinowitz on HPD hearing timing and financial review process

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I will note that your hearing was at four o'clock and to many tenants at Tracy Towers who have jobs made it impossible for them to attend in the community room or to testify online.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I would urge you

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: in the future, knowing that middle class, so many middle class and working class New Yorkers live in these Michelama developments, to have these hearings at a time that actually meets the needs of the residents who live there.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I want to talk about the fiscal.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So it's unclear.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So you have to submit.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I want to get this right.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You have to submit whatever the management company says they need the increase to be publicly, and then you go over what it should be.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We do an additional review.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I mean, it's a multi-step process.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So you review beforehand, before they propose it, you then present it at a public hearing.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And then you do an additional review.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But I just want a clarity.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Did you before, before the public hearing, did you review the financials?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Of course, we're constantly reviewing the financials of every Mitchell-Lama development.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I don't want to harp on the security costs, but it doesn't look to me like you went over the financials.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: beforehand.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I mean, that is a huge red flag, an increase of 50% for the security costs alone.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, it is a large increase, but we are

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: going over the expenses and we're going to continue to go over the expenses to see where we can cut.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz details Tracy Towers' $40.7M loan from 1971 accruing $106.2M in interest by 2012, and asks HPD to clarify the debt history. Julie Walpert explains a withdrawn federal subsidy led to the interest accrual, and HPD/HDC deferred payments to protect tenants.
chapters.title: Council Member Dinowitz on Tracy Towers loan history and accrued interest

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, I want to talk about capital needs.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I mean, brief history of Tracy Towers.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Tell me if any of this is off base.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: This is according to financial documents for Tracy Towers.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: In 19, it was 1971.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It was built with a $40.7 million loan.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It was a low interest, no interest loan.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That was part of the Michelin program.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And by 2012, Tracy owed the principal and an additional $106.2 million in interest.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Is that inaccurate?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I mean, I'll have to look more deeply into the existing debt on Tracy Towers.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So he's deferring and accruing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: So when the development was first being developed, there was supposed to be a federal subsidy that would bring down the interest rate.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: And prior to occupancy, that subsidy was withdrawn.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: So, you know, again, that predates me.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: I've been here forever, but it still predates me.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I guess not forever.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But the question is still, it started with a $40.7 million loan, and by 2012, there was $106.2 million

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: interest on that loan, plus the principal.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Right, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So the program that Julie was referring to subsidized the interest rate or the interest rate payments, is that right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And without that subsidy, that is the result.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Over the course, so it sounds like over the course of 50 years, HPD did nothing to try to lower those interest payments, didn't do anything to pay off the principal, and now residents today are paying the price.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I mean, I wouldn't agree with that statement, Julie.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That's why I'm asking you to please clarify the history.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And this is going to be my last because I'll come back for a second round, if that's okay, because I know there's a ton of other people.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: If we had addressed those arrears, it would have been on the tenants to address those arrears.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: And so what HPD and HGC work together to balloon those arrears, put them aside, not require any payment on them, and it will just remain affordable for 50 years at this point.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: But it was actually done that way to protect the tenants so that they wouldn't have rent increases to pay the debt service.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, I'm going to follow up on this later, but we have a ton of other people.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair, for the opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Hudson asks what accountability measures ensure loans and grants are used effectively and produce measurable improvements. Phillips describes the IPNA, architect/contractor process, and monthly HPD/lender construction monitoring.
chapters.title: Council Member Hudson on accountability for capital improvement loans

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, we have Councilmember Hudson followed by Councilmember Riley.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Just a couple of quick questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What accountability measures are in place to ensure that loans and grants awarded for Mitchell-Lama capital improvements are used effectively, completed on schedule, and produced?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Measurable improvements for residents?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So prior to construction, we require, like I mentioned before, the IPNA, which outlines the potential scope of work.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Then we have an architect work to develop the scope of work and a contractor that completes the scope of work.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: During construction, HPD and the private lender monthly go to the building to ensure that work is completed.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And then after that work is completed, payment is released to the contractor.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So every month there's the lender and HPD, if we have city capital in the project, monitoring construction to ensure that the capital is spent correctly.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And in accordance with the scope of work.

chapters.summary: Hudson says she'll follow up on Tivoli Towers' ongoing construction issues, and asks what HPD offers households that don't qualify for SCRI/DRI but can't afford increases. Phillips says Section 8 vouchers would be ideal but they're in shortfall.
chapters.title: Council Member Hudson on Tivoli Towers construction oversight and displacement prevention

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so that would obviously be the case at Tivoli Towers?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: That should, any project that HPD lends on and any private lender.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, so HDC as the private lender would do monthly walkthroughs for the construction period.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, it would be great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: My office will follow up with you directly on that because Tivoli Towers has had ongoing construction for years and continues to find new issues and concerns.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so I'll follow up on that one.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: For Mitchell-Lama households that do not qualify for scree and dree but still cannot afford rent increases, what additional assistance or policy options is HPD considering to help prevent displacement?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So ideally, we would be able to offer Section 8 vouchers to those households.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Fortunately, we're in shortfall, so we don't have that ability at the moment.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I would say the best thing we can do is advocate for additional federal resources so we have more Section 8 to offer tenants.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: That's it.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Riley asks how much money is specifically dedicated to city-supervised Mitchell-Lamas and how developments are selected. Phillips says they don't break out the budget separately and describes the development pipeline process.
chapters.title: Council Member Riley on capital funding dedicated to Mitchell-Lamas

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Riley, followed by Banks.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: Good afternoon, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: Thank you for being here today.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: I just want to share the same sentiments as Councilmember Dinowitz.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: I think Tracy Towers, a lot of residents are truly afraid right now.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: They don't know what's going to happen with these increases.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: So I think that's where you hear the passion and you see a lot of the residents that are here today.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: The administration has committed hundreds of millions of dollars in FY27 and FY28 for capital improvements.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: How much money is specifically dedicated to city-supervised Mitchell-Lamas, and how were those developments selected, and how much will that investment reduce projected resident increases?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we don't break out our total preservation budget by the Mitchell Lama stock versus other types of affordable housing stock that come through our pipeline.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Whenever there's a Mitchell Lama development in our development pipeline, we work as quickly as possible to close on financing.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Sometimes it can take some time.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Some quite some time because of the pre-development activities that are required to get ready for the loan closing.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Doing the IP&A for a large development,

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: usually takes quite some time.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Then you have to hire a design team to be able to put together the scope of work and drawings.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Then we have to get approvals from DOB.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The project has to be competitively bid to a contractor.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And then, as you can imagine, we have a lot of due diligence that we put people through to get through a loan closing.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So all of that can stretch out.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The development timeline for some folks, but we work in tandem and closely with the housing companies to get them through that development process as quickly as possible.

chapters.summary: Riley asks HPD to commit to providing a development-by-development capital needs assessment, and asks what protections ensure residents are involved before land is sold or developed. Phillips says co-op boards drive the process; Alika Amprey-Samuel emphasizes creative revenue tools.
chapters.title: Council Member Riley on capital needs assessment and land sale protections

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: Can HPD commit to providing the council with a development by development capital needs assessment for all city supervised Mitchell Llamas?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We have, and I think my colleague Alika said before, the total number of Mitchell Llamas where we have existing IPNAs.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we can take a look.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: We can provide you with a list that we have and work with your offices on the costs associated with the IPNAs.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: That would be a great.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: Your testimony discusses selling parking lots and undeveloped land to generate revenue.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: What protections will be placed to ensure residents are meaningfully involved before land connected to their development is sold or developed on?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, so we want to make sure that I think it would be a little bit different for the co-ops versus the rentals.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: For the co-ops, they would be completely driving that process, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The board of directors has a fiduciary responsibility to...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: ensure that they're doing capital planning and reporting back to the shareholders.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So that would be more of a collaboration directly with the shareholders and more up to the shareholders to see what they want done with their land if they have the space to develop.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: With the private rentals, I think the situation would be a little bit different, and we would make sure we would do outreach to work with the housing companies and engage the tenants.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So any undeveloped land is benefiting.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The existing residents in the development.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: I just want to actually add the fact that we're sitting here talking about the operating costs and some of the subsidies that we don't have access to right now, like the Section 8 vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And so we need to be creative on ways to be able to get more resources into these buildings.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And so if we're talking about the opportunity to develop on land, we need to really have those conversations in a thoughtful way.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And so I just wanted to lift that up because if we don't have tools, this could be one of them.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And we've seen this play out in other housing portfolios.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: And I think the council is ready to work with you guys on being innovative and coming up with those creative ways.

chapters.summary: Riley argues that if 30% increases are common across the portfolio, it demonstrates a systemic failure. Phillips agrees and points to J51 reauthorization and collaborative tools as solutions.
chapters.title: Council Member Riley on 30% increases as systemic failure

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: My last question is, you stated that Tracy Towers is not exceptional to this.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: But respectfully, isn't that the problem?

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: Because if 30% increase are becoming a common across portfolios, that demonstrates a systematic failure that we have within our system.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: So I think there are creative ways that we could think about improving this down the line, because I do think no one here believes that 30% increase in anything is something that's substantial.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, and we're happy to work together with folks to take a look at more creative ways to reduce operating expenses, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Like the reauthorization of J51 is a really good tool that we could use to layer into the 5% shelter rent tax to lower expenses.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So more collaboration on things like that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: Thank you, Deputy Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Kevin C. Riley
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Walpert
utterances.text: Banks followed by J.C.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Inches?

chapters.summary: Banks cites the state comptroller audit identifying mold, pests, and infrastructure deficiencies, and asks what specific investments the city has made and how HPD measures effectiveness. Phillips describes the preservation pipeline and addressing hazardous conditions first.
chapters.title: Council Member Banks on state audit findings and capital investment effectiveness

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: First of all, I want to thank the Speaker and the entire leadership in bringing this hearing together.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: On capital investment and preservation of Mitchell-Lama, recently there was an audit by the State Comptroller, Tom DiNapoli, which identified persistent,

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: unresolved issues across Mitchell-Lama developments, including mold, pest infestation, infrastructure deficiencies, and other hazardous conditions that directly impact residents'quality of life.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Given the significant capital needs facing many Mitchell-Lama properties, what specific investments has the city made to preserve and rehabilitate this housing stock,

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: particularly city-supervised developments?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And additionally, how has HPD measured the effectiveness of those investments?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And can the agency provide examples where capital funding has resulted in measurable improvements for residents?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, so, I mean, we're happy to work with every development that's in our pipeline and is willing to come into our pipeline to do a major capital improvement project.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: When we do a major capital improvement project, the goal is to cure underlying conditions, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we want to make sure that we're addressing the most hazardous and urgent conditions first, and then ensuring that we get to the most hazardous and urgent conditions first.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: to the rest of the work that's outlined in the IPNA and the scope of work.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And any examples that you can give us?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Of a successful loan closing and improved conditions.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I think we'll be able to, I mean, I don't have one off the top of my head unless, Julie, you do.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We're happy to provide samples.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: You can give that to the committee later.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Pass it down.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: All right, thank you.

chapters.summary: Banks asks who bears responsibility when deficiencies persist for years—management, boards, contractors, or HPD. Phillips describes HPD's oversight authority and providing technical assistance, noting differences between co-ops and rentals.
chapters.title: Council Member Banks on accountability for deteriorating Mitchell-Lama conditions

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: When it comes to accountability, the Mayor Madami campaign on vote affordability and accountability, particularly as it relates to housing,

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: when serious deficiencies are identified in Mitchell Lama developments and remain unresolved for years, who ultimately bears responsibility?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Is it the building management, the cooperative boards, the contractors, or the oversight agency themselves?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And more importantly, what concrete accountability measures has the administration implemented to ensure all parties are meeting their obligations and that both taxpayers and the residents are seeing tangible results

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: for every public dollar invested?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we have authority to oversee the Mitchell Lama developments, and part of that authority is to review the financials and ensure that the project is being managed appropriately.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Again, I think the situation is a little different when it comes to the co-ops versus the rentals.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: But obviously,

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We want to provide as much support and technical assistance as we have available to ensure that these developments don't get into this situation in the first place.

chapters.summary: Banks asks whether HPD views parking lot sales as innovative preservation tools or signs of deeper financial distress. Phillips calls it a massive opportunity and innovative revenue source. Alika Amprey-Samuel emphasizes collaboration.
chapters.title: Council Member Banks on asset sales as preservation tools vs. distress signals

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And when it comes to financial stability, a recent City Limits article highlighted a Michilama development in the East Village that is considering selling

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: a parking lot to generate revenue for capital improvements and raising operating costs.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Does HPD view these type of asset sales as innovative preservation tools or as a warning, a sign of deeper financial distress within the Michilama portfolio?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I'm a glass half full kind of guy, so I'm going to say that it's an opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It's a massive opportunity for the Mitchell Llamas to be able to use undeveloped land and come up with a completely different source of funding that we don't have available to us right now.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I would say it's incredibly innovative, and it's something that we would want to work with co-ops and private rental companies with, and in conjunction.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: In conjunction with the city council to ensure that it's equitable development.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: Can I just add one thing?

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: I know that just because your colleague just walked in and we had, and I wanted to say again, you'll hear me say this over and over.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: This is a collaborative effort.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: We have to do this together.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And thank you, council member, for having a conversation with our team about the development in your district.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And I get it.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: It is in my good friend's district, but there's obviously opposition to it as well.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And that's obviously been pointed out in the article.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: But nevertheless, I guess the conversation needs to be had because, you know, obviously parking is a large need throughout the city.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And this obviously, you know, we should definitely.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: What should be part of the conversation is continue to take the pressure off of the need for parking.

chapters.summary: Banks asks about HPD's oversight before Linden Plaza's rehabilitation and how conditions deteriorated so far, and calls for accountability for the management company that ran it into the ground. Phillips says Linden Plaza's outcome is positive. Alika notes HPD visited both Tracy Towers and Linden Plaza for lessons learned.
chapters.title: Council Member Banks on Linden Plaza oversight and management accountability

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And my last question, and hopefully the chair will indulge me, Linden Plaza, which is in my district, which is a city-supervised Mitchell Lama,

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: which is a Mitchell Lama development in my district and which currently is undergoing major rehabilitation.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Prior to these investments, residents endured deteriorating conditions for many years.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: As someone who regularly visited this development, knocked on many a doors, I personally observed the deterioration that was taking place in Mitchell Lama and the concerns that existed.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Going back to oversight, what oversight responsibilities did HPD have before this rehabilitation effort began?

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And how were these conditions allowed to deteriorate to such an extent before meaningful intervention had occurred?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, so our oversight authority, like I mentioned, is reviewing financials and providing some sort of technical assistance.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: and advice to the owners and to the management companies and to the shareholders.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I think the outcome of Linden Plaza is actually a really positive one.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: From what I've seen so far, maybe I'm talking out of line here, but I think where Linden Plaza got you today is a really good place compared to what it was.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And I would agree.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: It's definitely made a lot of progress in Linden Plaza.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: But also, too, the question of accountability, because the management company that ran Linden Plaza into the ground, we need to hold them accountable.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: And that's the reason why I'm bringing that up.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: There has to be some type of justice still exacted on those folks who put them in that condition by refinancing the property and not investing it into the development.

utterances.speaker: Chris Banks
utterances.text: Thank you, Madam Chair, for your indulgence.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: Madam Chair, can I just say one quick thing?

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: I just want to highlight that when the team at HPD and others visited Tracy Towers, we went intentionally.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: We left Tracy Towers and we went to Linden Plaza to look at just some lessons learned and best practices, and that was intentional that day.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: So I just wanted to actually highlight that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Justin Sanchez asks what tools HPD has to intervene at the management level when a single manager shows patterns of neglect across multiple buildings. Phillips describes bringing in property management firms for deeper reviews and mentions AEP and 7A programs.
chapters.title: Council Member Sanchez on management company neglect across multiple developments

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Jay Sanchez, followed by Councilmember Fedeus.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee, and thank you, Chair P.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Sanchez, for helping to put everything together today.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Howdy, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So a few questions.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So two Mitchell Lama developments in the South Bronx, Mins Plaza and OUB Houses.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Have numerous really hazardous violations.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And they're both run by the same management company.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So when a single manager across...

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Shows a pattern of neglect across multiple of their portfolio projects.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: What tools does HPD have to intervene at the management level rather than building by building?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So at a minimum, we would

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: bring in the property management firm to talk about the conditions, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Like I mentioned for Tracy Towers, we're bringing the property management firm in to discuss what exactly has happened in Tracy Towers and is it indicative across their entire portfolio.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We would be happy to call in the same property manager or whatever the property manager is at these two developments and have a conversation with them to do a much deeper dive than we have in the past.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Is there, so there's no automatic trigger or alert or notice that you get once a developer or a property owner receives a certain number of violations across a number of their portfolio projects?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we have HPD's code enforcement team has, you know, there's the AEP program, there's 7A, underlying conditions program.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Mitchell Llamas are not exempt from those programs.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks whether HPD can remove or disqualify a managing agent from Mitchell-Lama developments and what triggers that. Phillips confirms HPD has the authority to work with housing companies to find new management. Alika notes management companies are leaving the portfolio.
chapters.title: Council Member Sanchez on HPD authority to remove or disqualify managing agents

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Similarly, does HPD have the authority to remove

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: or disqualify a managing agent from Mitchell Lama Developments?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: If so, is there a threshold there that would trigger that?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we do have the authority.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I don't believe that there's a specific threshold, but we are able to work with the housing company to find a new property management firm if that's what the residents want.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And what the shareholders want.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Has there been, what is the history of that?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: What was the latest one?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: How frequent or unfrequent does this happen?

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: I actually just wanted to lift up that we're seeing just challenges with property management companies and managing agents across every housing portfolio that you can imagine.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And so whereas in the past we would deal with building specific issues, we are, as the first deputy commissioner mentioned, really working with the entire portfolio of the managing agent.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: But I also want to say that we are seeing more management companies leave out of the portfolio.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And there is a conversation of how do we get more property management companies to be interested in working with our portfolios.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And again, this is not just specific to Mitchell Lama.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: It's the entire, like what we're seeing across the housing industry when it comes to property management.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And can you talk to any relationships that you have with these agents?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I mean, a lot of the times they hide behind a lot, not particularly in Mitchell Lama, just hide behind these pseudo corporations that you can't get in touch with people and then you have to work with them.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: How has your experience been with these managing agents and being able to actually get responsiveness, delivery for residents, delivery for tenants, and ability for council members to then work with you and work with the managing companies to really get?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I mean, I think the majority of property management firms, and not just the Mitchell Llama stock, but the broader affordable housing stock, if we call them, they will come in to meet with us.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks how frequently HPD audits Mitchell-Lama books and raises concerns about a development with over $1M in shareholder arrears, asking whether rent increases become self-defeating. Phillips describes annual financial statement requirements and the limited operating subsidy tools available.
chapters.title: Council Member Sanchez on HPD audits and shareholder arrears

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And how frequently...

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Does HPD audit?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: the books of the city's Mitchell Lama developments?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We require annual financial statements and annual audits, and then our housing supervision team reviews the annual financials that are submitted.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And finally, research, if I may.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Research on my district shows I have a development that has over a million dollars in shareholder arrears.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: When arrears signal that the current changes are unaffordable, how does HPD couch further rent increases, such as those at Tracy Towers, with the program's purpose of affordability?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Is the agency identifying a point in which rent increases become almost self-defeating?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So the biggest operating subsidy source that we have, like I've said before, is Section 8, which is a federal source that we need to advocate together to get more of.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: That is our main, besides that, there's scree and tree.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Those are like the three main sources for operating subsidies.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We don't have, our city capital cannot be used as an operating source.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It's only for construction.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Farías delivers opening remarks about Jamie Towers in her district, citing systemic failures in oversight and management, and introduces Reso 83 urging stronger state protections for Mitchell-Lama residents.
chapters.title: Council Member Farías on Jamie Towers and systemic oversight failures

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Furtias, followed by Eldwell.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair Lee and Chair Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I really want to begin by being as clear as possible.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Michelama residents have waited far too long for meaningful accountability.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: State audits have repeatedly documented unsafe living conditions.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: financial mismanagement, and failures in oversight, while too many residents continue to struggle with unresolved complaints, deteriorating buildings, and significant carrying charge increases.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: It's why I'm also the prime sponsor of a resolution being heard today, Reso 83, urging the state to establish stronger protections and independence.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: and an oversight for Michilama residents, but today is really about the city's role.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We have an obligation to examine whether our own agencies are exercising the level of oversight and enforcement that residents deserve, and why these conditions have been allowed to persist despite years of documented warning signs.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: These failures are not abstract.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: They have real consequences for New Yorkers, as we've already heard from my colleagues, and in fact feel systemic.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: When Michilama developments fall into financial distress because of poor management, inadequate oversight, or years of delayed adjustments to carrying charges, residents are ultimately the ones who are paying the price.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Buildings deteriorate, essential repairs are deferred, and when the financial reality can no longer be ignored, residents are often confronted with steep and sudden increases that many, particularly

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: our seniors and those living on fixed incomes, simply cannot afford.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: This housing was supposed to be these steps to create generational wealth and home ownership that can then be passed on.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We saw this firsthand at Jamie Towers in my district where proposed increases threatened to displace long-term residents from the homes they had counted on remaining affordable.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: That experience underscored for me that we cannot continue responding only after a crisis has developed.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We need stronger oversight, earlier intervention, and greater accountability to protect both.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: The financial health of these developments and the residents.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: who call them home.

chapters.summary: Farías asks whether the administration has planned new reporting and monitoring safeguards to prevent situations like Jamie Towers. Phillips discusses J51, SCRI income cap expansion, and Section 8 advocacy.
