chapters.title: Council Member Farías asks HPD about new safeguards to prevent steep increases

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'm now going to turn to my line of questioning chair.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Has the administration put into inception or at least plan to provide new safeguards such as reporting and monitoring to ensure that the example I gave at Jamie Towers does not occur and maintenance charges can keep up with the requirements?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So like I said before, we continuously review every development's financials.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And as we, we're still looking for more tools in the operating expense toolbox, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: J51 paired with capital improvements, the J51 benefit could really go a long way to help mitigate the increase of rent or maintenance fees.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: That's where we want to continue to look for more resources and to try to get a little bit more innovative.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: On the income side, SCRI injury are tools that we already have.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The increase at the state level for SCRI from $50,000 to a $75,000 limit, once that's enacted on the local level, that should be able to help some of the seniors who are on fixed incomes.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: They might not have been eligible before, but they will be eligible going forward as long as they earn less than $75,000.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And then lastly, again, it's the question of the operating subsidy, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Like if we can advocate for more Section 8, that can ensure that households are better able to absorb the increases.

chapters.summary: Farías presses on the lack of manageable transition time for families facing steep increases, noting 30-day notice is insufficient. Phillips says HPD tries to stagger increases but the Tracy Towers increase hasn't taken effect yet.
chapters.title: Council Member Farías on 50% increases and lack of transition time for families

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: But I don't think this is only about those cases that you mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: There's what we're seeing happen across the board, and I know you folks are familiar with this, a 50% increase

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: in one fiscal year or really overnight for people, the same as what I'm seeing on my side of town with the financial, the lending capacity being extremely limited because of 20

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: or 25 years of a management company that is supposed to have the oversight of city agencies that are supposed to catch early on that boards are not approving a 2%

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: increase into coffers to ensure that in 10 years or 15 years when we need elevator replacement, roof replacement, modernization,

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: local laws that come into play that have to be implemented by the boards and by the Mitchell-Lama campuses themselves, there's money there to do that.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We're now doing increases where we're asking people overnight to catch up on 20 years of poor management from the city and the state, and also

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: no increases that they have enough coffers or have enough lending capacity to make the repairs or to implement the local laws that we're putting into play.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: What are you folks doing to look at how do we not do a 50% increase overnight and provide some stability or some flexibility to allow a long run of transition to get them to a place where they financially have capacity to stay in?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: their homes and also make the repairs that are necessary.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, so typically when we do increases with the city-supervised Mitchell-Lamas, and I think the Mitchell-Lama you might be referring to is a state-supervised.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I have both city and state.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: But, you know, similar conditions across both, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Different oversight, different authorities.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We try to stagger the increases as best we can, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We take a look at the operating expenses.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We take a look at the new debt that is taking care of the capital improvements.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We try to stagger the increases as best we can so it's a bit more manageable for households over a longer term, and so it's not just overnight.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay, so what happens in the case of Councilmember Eric-Dinowitz's district where there's a 50% increase at Tracy?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: That's not overnight.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: How many meetings did you folks have to have that increased role into play?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Like, when did they find out and how much did they know ahead of time that they were getting a 50% increase?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Oh, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The increase isn't in place yet.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I heard that earlier.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'm asking leading up to it.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: How much time was given to the families in Tracy Towers that they were going to get?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I mean, at a minimum, we had...

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: As obviously, like, I'm being a bit facetious with overnight, right?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: But, like, what's the time frame?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So there was the 30-day notice, which we're required to do.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: So not overnight, but just in one month.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The increase hasn't gone into effect yet.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I understand that.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: But still, right, when we have inflation, when we have an affordability crisis, even 30 days or three months is not going to be sufficient amount of time for families.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: You're asking people to find completely different careers or get promotions in a short amount of time.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And if they're retired or on a fixed income, I'm preaching to the choir on this dais, and I'm sure it's the crowd, but it's still not a manageable amount of time for such an increase.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And I think we all can agree on that.

chapters.summary: Farías asks whether HPD is required to help close out projects stalled by lack of project managers, citing Jamie Towers' $8M in allocated capital stuck for three budget cycles without a project manager. Phillips says they're staffing up the preservation finance team.
chapters.title: Council Member Farías on HPD project manager assignments and pipeline delays

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: A few more questions, if I may, chairs.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Is HPD required to help close out projects at Michilama Housing Developments that are unable to do so due to lack of resources and personnel?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Often in my district, members designate capital dollars, but their projects are stuck on hold waiting for a project manager from HPD.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: In light of this and with some of the projects stuck for multiple years,

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Will the administration be working closely to close those projects for our Michilamas and by what means and methods?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So by close out, I'm taking that to understand to close on construction financing, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So as part of the housing plan and...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We've been able to staff up our preservation finance team with a lot more staff, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So if we have more staff in our preservation finance team, we're able to assign more projects to them and work through the pipeline more quickly.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So with that additional staff and with additional capital resources, we should be able to move more Mitchell Llamas through the development pipeline more quickly.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Have you guys projected how many projects you have outstanding and if the personnel is going to be able to assist with getting those done and what's the timeline fashion?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we have, I believe it's 12 Mitchell Llamas in our pipeline right now, which means they're gearing up and getting close to closing.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Every Mitchell Lama and every, you know, regardless if it's a Mitchell Lama or not, every project has its own.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: unique development timeline based on the capital needs, the scope of work, and the development team that's working together to get to the construction loan closing.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So depending on the capacity of the board or of the owner of the Mitchell Lama, that can play a little bit into the timeline.

chapters.summary: Farías asks whether HPD has a central tracker and how Mitchell-Lamas are prioritized in closings. Phillips describes an internal tracker and says prioritization depends on project readiness. Farías notes Jamie Towers has financing in place but no project manager assigned.
chapters.title: Council Member Farías on HPD project tracking and Mitchell-Lama prioritization

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Does HPD have a central tracker of all their projects that are in the works and that could have, that are publicly funded in this way?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We have an internal tracker of projects that are in our pipeline and then we report out on projects once construction closing has happened.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And does the prioritization of HPD

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: closings, how does that include Mitchell's?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Does it just come down to the management, the timeline of the project, if it's fully financed?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, it comes down to whether or not the project is ready.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And our project managers work with the development team and the owner to get them as ready as possible, as quickly as possible.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I only ask because, granted,

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Jamie Towers is a state Mitchell, but HPD is the one that, from the money that I allocate through my capital budget to

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Jamie Towers, they're dependent on HPD's assigning them a project manager in order to move the process along.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And overall, in my district, I've dedicated nearly...

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: $12 million in capital dollars to modernization and repairs on Michilama campuses, but I've given Jamie Towers four over my first term.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: and my assembly member recently allocated another four to them, but they have not been able to move any of that money over the last three budget cycles because HPD has not assigned them a project manager.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And so I'm, you know, for me, that's a hindrance to how the progress

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: of that project can move and whether or not people can move freely within the campus themselves.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And I'm just interested in understanding if the money is all there, their financing is in place, but we've gone three closing cycles without

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: getting them a project manager and on the closing list, what more does a campus like Jamie Towers need to prove that they need their project to be completed and started?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, and we thank you for the support with the ResoA funds and every council member, I think, does their best to put as much towards the Mitchell Lama as possible and we're very grateful for that.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We can look more into the timeline for a project manager being assigned, but like I said, we're in the process of staffing up.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We were able to get a lot of new hires, and we're working as quickly as we can to get them on board.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And then once they're on board, to ensure the project is actually ready to close.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: If ResOA is assigned to a project, it does not necessarily mean that everything is in place for a construction loan closing.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: There could be ResOA available and a project manager unassigned.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Not great, but on the development team side, the ownership side, they might not have done any of their due diligence or prepare for a closing as well.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So it's a little bit of a give and take of ensuring we're getting somebody assigned and helping the owner of the co-op board drive the project to a construction loan closing.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Well, I'd love to continue that conversation offline now with this.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Campus itself having $8 million from the state and the city, I hope we're in a better place to move that along.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I will follow up with the additional questions I have because I've gone over my time, but thank you to both of the chairs and thank you folks.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Just as a reminder for folks that are here from the public, if you haven't signed up to testify, if you could sign up with one of the sergeants in the back, that'd be great.

chapters.summary: Aldebol asks about HPD oversight to ensure allocated funds are used appropriately and projects move faster, noting costs escalate with delays. Phillips says the referenced project is in the pipeline and new project managers will help move things along.
chapters.title: Council Member Aldebol on capital funding accountability and project delays

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Okay, so we have Councilmember Aldebar followed by Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Thank you for testifying today.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: 1950 Hutchison Parkway Apartments, it's a co-op, a Chalamet co-op in my district in the East Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: In the FY27 budget, I worked to secure funding to cover one of the major capital projects.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Of the apartment complex and by taking that large maintenance cost off, that capital cost off the books of the property,

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: you know, the goal is to decrease the need for major maintenance.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: increases.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: So my question is kind of in line with Councilmember Farias.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: What accountability measures does HPD have in place as part of your oversight to ensure that these savings are passed on to residents?

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: And the controller's report included findings about financial oversight failures across

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: New York City and New York State management of the Lama properties.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: City Council report also cited returned funding from past fiscal years due to mismanagement and money not used despite the obvious need for capital improvements.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: What oversight measures does HPD have to ensure that money allocated towards specific projects are used?

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Appropriately and how can we move these projects through the pipeline faster

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: because we can allocate money but if it takes years for these projects to happen the costs just go up and then we never, it feels like this cycle that we never

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: can get over.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: So just if you have any ideas of how to move that, you know, time is money also.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: So how, you know, what ideas do you have to get these projects moved more timely so that

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: the costs don't keep

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: escalating over time.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So thankfully, that project that you referenced is in the pipeline.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yes, I know.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The additional project managers that we have at HPD and that are currently being hired and onboarded will do a lot to move through the development pipeline.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Like I said, it's not just Mitchell Llamas that will benefit.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It's going to benefit all of the affordable stock that's coming through the preservation pipeline.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The amount of capital that we put into a project, obviously it's going to...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It's going to improve the conditions of the building, and it'll probably lead to reduced repairs and maintenance expenses in the short to medium term, which could help mitigate the increase that the residents will have to pay.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The other tools that we have available to us are trying to get more creative on the operating expense side.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So like I said about the J51 reauthorization, that will be really helpful to pair with capital improvements to ensure that the increases are not as high as they would have been without J51.

chapters.summary: Aldebol asks about HPD partnerships with Department for the Aging and senior organizations for SCRI outreach, given many Mitchell-Lamas are naturally occurring retirement communities. Alika describes 25 SCRI outreach workshops in the past year and willingness to partner with community organizations.
chapters.title: Council Member Aldebol on SCRI outreach for senior-heavy Mitchell-Lama developments

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: My other question is really about

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Getting people who are eligible for SCRE, DRE, or any other benefits, have you worked with other agencies?

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: I know that in the Michilama developments in my district, they have become naturally occurring retirement communities.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: We have a lot of seniors who live in Michilama.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: And, you know, are you working with the Department for the Aging?

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Are you working with other organizations that, you know, work with our seniors to make sure that they're getting all the financial assistance that they need to stay

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: in place?

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: So we are working with outside stakeholders, community-based organizations when we can.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And I said earlier, I'm so thankful to the council members who have reached out and worked with us to do workshops.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And some of them have done office hours.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And our teams have worked with the council members to actually go through applications with their constituents.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And I will also say that.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: Over the past year, we have done 25 SCRI outreach workshops just over the past year.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And so we're always willing to partner with community-based organizations that make sense.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Do you have a sense of how many?

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: many people are maybe eligible for a scree or dree and have not applied.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: And if the threshold, the income threshold is increased to 75,000, how many more could that represent?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I don't think we have that.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Projection available right now.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: But when an increase is going to be scheduled, like my colleague Alika had mentioned, we will do everything we can to do outreach and to engage seniors to ensure that they understand the eligibility requirements.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Again, the pre-filled applications are very helpful and we'll work as best as we can to get households signed up.

chapters.summary: Aldebol discusses a Mitchell-Lama co-op that received a huge increase after 10 years of none, and argues for regular gradual increases instead of waiting. Phillips agrees HPD would welcome regular increases and notes co-op boards often resist raising maintenance.
chapters.title: Council Member Aldebol on gradual increases vs. deferred maintenance cycles

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: And rather than have, you know, smaller percentage increases over time, I mean, one of the, it's not New York City, but we have a Michilama co-op that just got

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: a tremendously huge increase after 10 years of not having increases.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: You know and their you know their response to me was well we we've had to do all these capital improvements and we but you know it's like why did we get to this point

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: where now they're scrambling to do all these capital improvements

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: And they obviously have to balance their books.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: And instead of doing it on a year, you know, balancing their books on a yearly basis.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: and having a more, you know, regular schedule of, you know, maintenance and, you know, and capital improvements or whatever needs to happen in that building.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Like, you know, I've been in Tracy Tower many times.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: I used to represent the workers there.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Yeah, I think that's a change that we were willing.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: But, you know, there are, you know, wage increases and benefit increase for the staff, you know, for the workers there.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: We're not going to, like, not raise wages and benefits over time.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: We're not going to wait 10 years for people to get a wage increase.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: I mean, that has been happening.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: You know, over time and the needs and the recurring maintenance needs, you know, happen over time and waiting 10 years

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: to fix the problem on the backs of tenants is, you know, really unacceptable.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, I would say as like a former underwriter for affordable housing, like we would welcome regular increases at Mitchell Lamas if they came to us with the request, right, to have a

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: little bit of a stable instead of waiting until we're not meeting our expenses, the capital needs are out of control, and we're in a crisis moment.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Brewer raises concerns about Mitchell-Lama conversions to HDFC, asks about Clinton Towers' construction problems, and calls for a roundtable with state and city stakeholders. Phillips says no current conversions are underway and offers to host a roundtable.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer on Mitchell-Lama to HDFC conversions and Clinton Towers

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, Councilmember Brewer, just before you ask your questions, I just wanted to address Councilmember Aldable that citywide,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: not with respect just to the folks living in Michilama, but we have 158,214 households that are eligible for scree or jury, but only 67,132 households received the benefit in 2023.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So Councilmember Dinowitz passed a great bill to try to bridge that gap, but it's a really big gap in service for people.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you, Julie Walpert, for 30 years of help, or 40 years.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I lost track.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't know anybody else, but I do appreciate all of Julie's work.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And also, you know, Jackie and Katie and Dick Heiler and everybody who's been doing this.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Bob Willis, may he rest in peace.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: My question is twofold.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: First of all, I was not a big supporter of Camden Towers going from Mitchell Lama to HDFC.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I hope that's not going to happen in the future.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Can you update me?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, we don't have any Mitchell Lamas at the moment that are trying to go from 2 to 11.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We don't have any that are trying to go from 2 to 11.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But are you interested in getting rid of the policy altogether?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Because this is supposed to be about affordability.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: I mean, I would just actually say that I don't think we are in a position to say that policy should go away because I would always put it back on the residents and the shareholders and what the people actually want in their buildings.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And when you speak to some people there, they feel that it was a...

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: I know, but I'm just saying we also...

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: Say that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't agree with it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We have to be, you know, think bigger because people are, want to make money.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I understand that, but I still think it should return.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I want that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Maybe we'll put it in writing and try to pass a law because we don't want any more conversions like that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Number two.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Changes in 2021 have, have slowed down the amount of conversions.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I still worry about the future.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Lots of same situations.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I've got, let's see, I've certainly got Trinity House, and they want to postpone their July 29th because they have such a large increase being projected.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Same problem.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I'll contact you, but I want to have more discussion about these things because it's huge.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Even though they haven't had it in a while and it's not a bad owner, it still needs to be postponed.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So we need to do that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Clinton Towers, a mess.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So that's the same issue.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Who's overseeing the construction?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Every single one.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: single day I get calls from the residents.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How does that pan out?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Why is it?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I think we need, and I suggest this to the chairs also, we need like a round table, everybody sitting in the room, state, city, those of us who have Mitchell Lamas.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I've been doing this work since 1978.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's how long I've been doing Mitchell Lamas.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And it has to be a discussion.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We want Mitchell Lama 2.0.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But we can't figure out 1.0 yet.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I would love to see this roundtable take place.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But meanwhile, Clinton Towers is a mess.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The board is a mess.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The management is a mess.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: All my friends, I know them all.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But it's not working.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So how do we handle something like that?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The balconies, the lobby, the money, everything.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The Con Ed bill.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about 110 West End Avenue's large increase and how HPD is addressing the state comptroller audit findings. Phillips explains Lincoln-Amsterdam hasn't had an increase in 25 years and needs to pay bills, and says HPD has prepared responses and met with the comptroller.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer on Lincoln-Amsterdam increases and state audit response

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I spent hours on the Con Ed bill.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Can I ask a question about the 19...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Has there been a roundtable since 1978?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I mean, we're happy to host a roundtable.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Not since Bob Willis was alive, to be honest with you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: That's good to know.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So anyway, Clinton Towers, what's an example of how do we...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Get that so that the construction is taking place and there's less angst.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Any suggestions?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, we currently do.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we have current weekly calls because it's in our pipeline, correct?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And we have weekly calls to prepare for a closing.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we have a project manager that's working with the development team to get to a construction loan closing as quickly as possible.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, we can have further discussions.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's very, very challenging.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: 110 West End Avenue, also with a huge increase as a Mitchell Lama.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: co-op in this case.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So is that something that you're working on?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How would I be helpful or how could I be helpful?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So it's not such a huge increase.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So Lincoln, Amsterdam, they haven't had a maintenance increase in 25 years.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And the maintenance is affordable to below 30% of area median income.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And with the increase, it will continue to be affordable to 30% area median income.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And they can't pay their bills.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So it's not a construction issue.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It's not that they don't need capital.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It's just they need expense money to pay their bills.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, so you're thinking that it's an okay increase at this point.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: 30% is large.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It doesn't matter for anyone.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It's a large increase, but they need to do it.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The board, actually, it's a co-op, and the board came forward with asking for this because they...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Yeah, but there's still angst in the building.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: As you know, every single co-op has a co-op board, and then they have the shareholders, and they don't always get along, right?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You know that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So you can say about the board, and I know that board very well, but that's something to always be aware of, are the shareholders versus the board.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Right, but they're doing their fiduciary responsibility in this case.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, all right.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then the report that the state controller, which was mentioned earlier, Clinton Towers is one of the disasters on that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: How are you addressing that report that this controller came up with?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we put together responses for the comptroller report.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And we actually had a follow-up meeting this morning on one of the audits with the state comptroller.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: All right, so you're going to share that with us, particularly if it has to do with our buildings?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, I think our response is foible.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then finally, roundtable, you'll host it, you'll invite the state, and we'll all be there.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I'd love to.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'll pay for coffee.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Holding you to that.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Nice.

chapters.summary: Wong asks whether Article 5 redevelopment co-ops like Penn South are eligible for HPD capital funds, noting they believed they were excluded. Phillips confirms they are eligible to apply through HPD's preservation pipeline.
chapters.title: Council Member Wong on Article 5 co-op eligibility for capital funds

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Councilmember Wilson, followed by Epstein.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So I appreciate the commitment and attention to Michelamos, particularly as outlined in the block-by-block plan and the millions of capital dollars committed.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I do have a question about Article 5 co-ops.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So I have in my district Penn South, which is nearly 3,000 units of the housing.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: It's often mistaken for a Mitchell Loma, but it's actually not.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: It's governed in a very similar way.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: But the way the provisions are written right now, they don't have access to the capital funds.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So I'm curious, how do Article 5 redevelopment co-ops fit into the plan for...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So they can apply through HPD's preservation pipeline like any other affordable housing development.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Regardless of, you know, incorporated under Article 2, Article 11, Article 5, they're eligible for city capital funds.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we would urge the development to put together a loan application with an IPNA to work.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Through our pipeline.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So they're not excluded.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Not to my knowledge, no.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: That's good news.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: They thought they were.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: There's a win.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: That's it.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I just had one question.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Epstein asks why HPD waits for Mitchell-Lamas to request increases rather than proactively recommending small annual increases. Phillips says HPD does make recommendations but co-op boards often resist. Epstein urges HPD to collaborate with council members to encourage boards.
chapters.title: Council Member Epstein on proactive rent increase recommendations

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Councilman Rupstein?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yeah, I want to just go back to what you said earlier about waiting to hear from Mitchell Lamas who haven't done an increase in 20 or 30 years.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm wondering why wait instead of just...

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Reach out to them every year saying, hey, you know, we advise you to do at least a 2% increase, so ways you can do it over time, instead of having to see, like, Councilmember Dinowitz saw, you know, a 30% or 50% increase.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I have a Michelama that had a 10%, 15%, 20% increase year after year after year, and that it's impossible for the residents to manage.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Why not take an affirmative position to try to get them to apply for those increases and make a constant, like, a 2% every year?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we do make recommendations, right, to folks.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Once we get the financials and we do a review, we make a recommendation about whether or not they should increase their rents or maintenance fees.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: As somebody who worked with HDFC co-ops for nearly eight years, it is sometimes very hard to get a co-op board to raise their own maintenance fees to cover expenses and...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Cover capital needs.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah.

chapters.summary: Epstein asks about getting shelter rent tax to zero and whether HPD will advocate for it. Phillips points to J51 as the best tool to get close to zero. Epstein asks about forgivable loans; Phillips says they're structured as deferring and accruing with a balloon.
chapters.title: Council Member Epstein on shelter rent tax reduction to zero and J51

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So I represent hundreds of HDFC co-ops.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I represent four Mitchell Lamas.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I've never heard from HPD saying, hey, we're really recommending this Mitchell Lama do an increase.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Can you help us set up a meeting with them?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Can you encourage the board to propose an increase?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Why not?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: collaborate with your partners in government to make sure that happens.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I mean, we would be happy to collaborate with anybody.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I would love that because, you know, we know our municipal alimony.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We talk to them regularly.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We don't see their finances, so we don't know what they're doing.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: The only time we hear about it is when there's a proposed increase and they're asking for 20% and everyone's, oh, my God, this is crazy.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We would rather see a 2% increase every year to ease it out over time.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: That's easier for folks.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, and I think under this administration, we'd be happy to reach out a little bit more proactively.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And it sounds like with the Gale-Brewer's roundtable, we can talk about all these amazing issues.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Dying for a roundtable.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm looking forward to it.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I want to be there, so please invite me to it.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Can ever these Mitchell Alamas get to 0% tax?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I know we talked about, you know, I'll take the win on the reduction from 10% to 5% of the shelter rent tax, because we did that last year in Albany.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We wanted to get to zero, but, you know, we'd love to see the administration support that next year to go to zero.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I hope HPD will be on board.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: But are there other ways to get to zero through J51?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, so you took the words right out of my mouth.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We can't get quite to zero with J51, but we can get as close as possible depending on the capital work.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yeah, any other available options for you to get closer to zero?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Not, I don't think, but besides J51, I think that's the biggest tool in our toolkit at the moment to get to as close to zero.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And is there a reason we can't get other tools in your toolkit to help you get to zero besides making them put lots of money into capital improvements to get it to zero?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: This could be a topic for the roundtable.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I love this.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: All right, I'm going to keep going if I can.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Just a couple more.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Okay, sorry about that.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And so why not put capital dollars on the table?

chapters.summary: Epstein asks why HPD doesn't match council members' Reso A capital dollars and why not offer forgivable loans. Phillips says HPD usually puts in far more than matching amounts and the loans defer and accrue. Epstein raises it as a roundtable topic.
chapters.title: Council Member Epstein on matching capital dollars and forgivable loans for Mitchell-Lamas

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: us to use our res away money why not have HPD matches we put a million you put a million and we all walk away happy why not do that we do that for night show

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: we do that government money comes in for night show we put money in for night show why not like me just halfway and do some free money for Mitchell Alamas too

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, so we usually do far more than a couple million dollars, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: When we do a preservation outcome or a larger recapitalization, we're usually putting in a lot of city capital.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: But this is loans, not grants.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: They're loans, but they're structured on what the project is able to pay.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So I think in the case of Tracy Towers, we're not requiring any payments on the HPD debt.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It'll defer and accrue.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So you're willing to give forgivable loans in these situations?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: They're not forgivable.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: They'll balloon at the end, but there's no principal and interest payments due.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And why not do forgivable loans?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I don't think that's within our...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I mean, that's a larger question that I don't know if I'm fully able to answer at the moment.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Another roundtable conversation.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I love this.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Roundtable, too.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: More opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'd love to see the city give more money to help Mitchell Llamas.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We know they need the money.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We want to help them as much as possible.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm just a little concerned that we're not using all the tools in our tool belt.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Did you say you're going to help advocate next year to reduce the shelter rent tax to zero?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I didn't know if you sent that response.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I don't think I said that.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Can you say that?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I don't think I said that.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Will you say that?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: J51 is a...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: A really, really good tool to pair with capital improvements.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: It sounds like another conversation to have.

chapters.summary: Epstein asks how HPD balances the need for more affordable housing with preserving Mitchell-Lama land value. Phillips says it will be case-by-case, looking at available land and capital needs in collaboration with the council and co-op boards.
chapters.title: Council Member Epstein on balancing affordable housing development on Mitchell-Lama land

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And I wanted the last question.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I know Councilmember Banks flagged the issue of potential development projects on...

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Our Mitchell Lama land.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm wondering how you balance the issues of needing more affordable housing versus the potential value of that land.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So how do you make that determination about the need for the affordability, because we need more affordable housing, and the need for making sure the Mitchell Lama stay whole?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: What is that decision process internally?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, I think it's going to have to be a case-by-case basis.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We have to look at the available land in each Mitchell Lama.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Not every Mitchell Lama is going to have a ton of development space available.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So I think looking at the capital needs, the available land that we could develop on, I think everyone might have to be its own unique snowflake.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: But in collaboration with the city council and if it's co-op with the board, I think we can get to a really good place to ensure that there's additional funds going to mitigate increases and pay for capital improvements outside of the city budget.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Well, thank you, and I appreciate the chair's indulgence.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm looking forward to this exciting roundtable.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Are you bringing snacks?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Always snacks.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: They're going to be vegan.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You okay with that, right?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: As long as it's free.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you for hanging in there with us.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks what indicators HPD receives regularly beyond financial statements and how they assess risk. Phillips cites financials, vacancy reports, outstanding payables, and online violation profiles as key indicators.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez on financial indicators and risk assessment for Mitchell-Lamas

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: That concludes our first round of questions.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We'll have a second round.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So I just want to wrap up a couple of more questions that I had.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And just to kind of recap, right, I feel like every single council member has said a version of the same thing.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Maintenance increases should be gradual.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We want to be a partner.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We want to help so that we're not seeing dramatic increases that are making up for multiple years at a time.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So more to come at the roundtable, but we certainly want to be a partner on that.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And just a follow-up, you said, so HPD is receiving annual financial statements.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Is there any...

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Are there other indicators that HPD is receiving regularly from Michilama properties?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And how are you taking that information into account in terms of assessing risk?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, so the financials are the key indicator of the financial health and sometimes the physical health of the building.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We also get a vacancy report, outstanding payables.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We can also see online, you know, what the violation profile looks like.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks what HPD offers co-ops struggling with shareholders in arrears and governance issues. Alika describes training and technical assistance for boards, and notes HPD has requested additional resources for capacity building.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez on HPD support for co-ops with shareholder arrears and governance challenges

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And on the topic that's perfect, I'll wait to my next question, is outstanding payables.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: One of the things that we hear about a lot in Kingsbridge Arms, for example, is that there's a lot of shareholders that are behind on their maintenance fees.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: It's an issue at HDFCs and just so many co-ops.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: What does HPD have to offer these co-ops that are having these governance challenges and working with their shareholders?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, so we're...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Do you want to...

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So our...

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: Housing Supervision team will provide actual training, technical assistance training, working directly with the boards on how to govern their buildings and what to look for.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And so we do that in-person training.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: We do virtual training.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: We have online webinars available.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And so we're constantly looking for other opportunities to partner with organizations to do that.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And I also, because we're sitting here, just want to lift up the fact that we have asked for resources and funding to be able to really get into building the capacity of these boards and work with our managing partners.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: We're doing all we can, but we would also like some resources to do more.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Okay, let us know how.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: You know the deal.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We're always here to advocate for those funds.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I had a specific case.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: It's not a Michilama.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: It is an HDFC.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: But I think the issue carries over in terms of a building that was not able to submit, for whatever reason, their property registration, that has a lot of shareholders that are in arrears, that has wanted to take folks to court and seek an eviction.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Julie and I were speaking two days ago with members of the team about this very question.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks about legal support resources for co-ops and raises concerns about a Mitchell-Lama placed in the Alternative Enforcement Program. Phillips says HPD doesn't offer legal resources but can connect to nonprofits, and says AEP signals a need for preservation intervention.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez on legal support for co-ops and AEP placement of Mitchell-Lamas

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: In addition to technical assistance, is there legal support that HPD makes available?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Are there partners that offer that, or is this a gap that our city has?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, so we don't offer those types of...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: of legal resources, but there are not-for-profit legal resource providers out there that hopefully we can connect those folks to.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Any you can name so I can go back and tell?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I think we can share that after if that's okay, if any come up in my memory.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It's been a while.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: No problem.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We can share it and we have a whole...

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Requests and ask for it.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: AEP and Mitchell Lamas.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I want to make sure to get this on the record today.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I represent a Mitchell Lama that is in the Alternative Enforcement Program, which makes absolutely no sense from the perspective of wanting to preserve affordable housing, but then we're, you know, as a city

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: giving these buildings all these violations and increasing their debts to the city and their financial responsibilities.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: What is the position of the administration on Mitchell Lamas and affordable developments being in the AEP program?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I mean, so there's a, when you look at the development side and the pipeline of projects coming through HPD and then the code enforcement side, there is,

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: and I think there always has been kind of a pretty strong firewall to ensure that the housing maintenance code is being enforced appropriately.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: AEP, when a building's in AEP, it's a really good indication that a larger preservation and stabilization outcome needs to happen.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And we should be able to divert, not divert, but apply resources to those.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: types of projects to get them out of AEP.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Look forward to more conversation on that.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks whether HPD has an accounting of how much increases are due to capital vs. operating costs. Phillips says it's almost always operating expenses, as HPD structures debt so residents don't pay principal/interest. Sanchez asks what happens to the balloon; Phillips says it's refinanced with extended affordability.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez on cost drivers for carrying charge increases and balloon loan outcomes

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And then my final question is coming from some of our friends in advocacy is, does HPD have an accounting for how much of carrying charges increases are due to capital expenditures versus

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: more of the operating costs?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So you mentioned utilities, you mentioned insurance, but do you have a sense of the relative reason for the cost drivers?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So not off the top of my head, Julie.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, I was going to say it's almost always just the operating expenses.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We have the ability to structure our debt in a way where the residents don't have to pay towards our debt service payments, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we'll defer and accrue principal and interest, and there's a balloon at the end.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And in that way, it mitigates the increase on maintenance and rents.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And one-on-one question, what happens to the balloon?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: At the end?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I mean, it gets rolled out.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Usually we refinance it.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: It's refinancing in exchange for affordability restrictions for longer.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, that's exactly right.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks HPD to explain how the debt works at Tracy Towers, noting the original $40M loan grew with $106M in interest and was refinanced to $147M. Phillips explains the difference between HDC's first mortgage requiring payments and HPD's deferred subsidy.
chapters.title: Council Member Dinowitz on Tracy Towers debt structure and loan history

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Councilmember Dinowitz.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Well, I had similar questions about the debt.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But first...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm also excited for the roundtable.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I will be relying on Council Members Epstein and Brewer for snacks.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I would just also say that limited equity co-ops like the Amalgamated Cooperative should be included.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: not Michelama, but in the same sphere, right?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So that it functions similarly and they have some of the similar issues.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So I would say that they should be part of this roundtable as well.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I need you to help explain how the debt works, because as I was starting in my first round,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Tracy had a $40 million loan.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The interest was $106 million.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It was refinanced to about $147 million.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm hearing you testify, well, we can't forgive the loan, but we just roll the loan over and it's no interest and it keeps growing.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That's what I'm hearing.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It keeps growing.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And at no point ever in the future do those payments ever have to be made.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Is that accurate?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we use the, if principal and interest payments aren't being made on any portion of our existing debt, there's a balloon at the end of the loan term.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So after 30 years, our balloon is there.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And as Councilmember Sanchez so eloquently put, it's our hook, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So by us having existing debt, and if it's a large balloon, then we're able to ensure that the project will stay within an affordable program for the longer term.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So none of the rent increases or maintenance increases are due to the need to pay off any of these loans, any of this debt?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: For Tracy Towers, the HPD debt that is in the project, none of the increase is going to pay towards the new capital that HPD put into the project.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, so I'm not talking about the new capital.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm talking about the new capital.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: About like the old capital, right?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The, all of that interest that accrued over the years, they don't, I'm hearing different things.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: They don't have to pay it back.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm just trying to understand if any of that does have to do with the rent increases for Tracy.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So different lenders, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: HPD has its own city capital that's contributed to the project.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And then there's an existing, there's a, sorry, I don't fully have the debt breakdown with me.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: But HTC has...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Has a first mortgage and that one gets paid off.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So typically when we structure our loans, there's usually a private lender and that private lender will require principal and interest payments and then HPD comes in with subsidy and those subsidy payments

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: or that subsidy doesn't require principal and interest payments and the loan defers and accrues and there's a balloon at the end of the 30 years.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So the debt that's in first position, that requires debt service payments.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The city capital we put in does not require debt service payments.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: All right.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz details a 2016 loan for elevator, boiler, and roof repairs at Tracy Towers where work was apparently not completed, and asks what oversight HPD conducts. Phillips describes monthly construction monitoring by HDC and HPD, and distinguishes between repair and replacement scopes.
chapters.title: Council Member Dinowitz on oversight of 2016 Tracy Towers capital loans and incomplete work

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Can you provide a breakdown of the city capital versus the private capital for...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Tracy and all of the Michelamas, because we were told, I think, that the rent increase is due in large part to the debt service.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Otherwise, the building is underwater.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But that will be for later.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The second question is about other loans that have been taken out and HPD's oversight over those loans for capital improvements.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: In 2016, there was a $40 million loan taken out for elevator repairs, elevator upgrades, boiler, roof.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I mean, you were in Tracy Towers.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The elevators have clearly not been upgraded.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That was in 2016.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The roof is leaking, so it either wasn't done or wasn't done well.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So I'm asking about the oversight.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: What oversight does HPD actually conduct to ensure that the money that is being borrowed that eventually has to be paid back by the tenants, that that work is actually being done?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And being done at a quality that they're not paying for just a few years later with leaky roofs and broken elevators.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, so monthly we monitor construction.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And in this case, it sounds like there was a...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: HTC was the private first lender, and they would go out and do monthly inspections to ensure that the work was completed.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Once the construction monitor or excuse me, construction person from HTC and the architect sign off on the work that was completed, then the payment is made to the contractor.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So every month of those documents, every month somebody is going out to the building.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And then every month a requisition is submitted, and that's how the contractor gets paid for work that was completed.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, are those documents public?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And please share those documents with us, where HDC or the contractor said this work was completed.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Completed.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Do you have those documents available to you?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I mean, I don't know if we have them available at the moment.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: HDC is a different agency than HDC.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: No, I understand, but you work in collaboration for the purpose of supporting people at our Mitchell Amas.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah, and we could see what they have.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We should see copies of those because the work wasn't done, right?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We have the documents that say the money was borrowed for...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: maintenance, oh sorry, it's rehabilitation.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There was 1.6 for an elevator upgrade, 2.4 for the boiler roof, 600,000 for elevator modernization.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And if you're testifying that someone has to sign off on that every month and the work wasn't done, that means someone did something wrong.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I also want to note, since I am testifying, the scope of work is very specific, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So an elevator repair upgrade is not an elevator replacement.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: That scope of work, those are two very different things.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Roof repairs is very different than roof replacement.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So those scope of work items are...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Completely different.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But I don't think any private homeowner would accept a job, would accept work where they completed repairs on the roof and that work and the roof the next year is leaking.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And we shouldn't accept that as a city either.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And that's why I'm interested in getting these documents.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I understand it's not your agency, but we are in the same city and the same city government.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And if we're all working together.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks about the $36M capital plan for Tracy Towers, when work begins, and what HPD can do to expedite permits. Phillips says work should start in the next couple months and HPD communicates with DOB to expedite. Dinowitz urges faster action given shed costs.
chapters.title: Council Member Dinowitz on $36M Tracy Towers capital plan and permit delays

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: for the betterment of the residents of our Michelama developments, we should be all beginning those documents.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And if it's happening at Tracy, it's happening at all of the Michelamas and many of the Michelamas around our city where work is done and there's no oversight as to the quality of work.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Now, there is a $36 million capital plan.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I believe the work was, when you came to visit, the work was supposed to start in June.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: The loan closing was in June.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Loan closing.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And when does the work begin?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So probably in the next couple months, I would say.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
