utterances.text: For any construction project, Mitchell Lama or not, after construction loan closing, there's a period where the construction crew and the contractor will continue to pull permits, continue to get all their paperwork in order, and then mobilize to be able to start.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Construction work.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And that's typical across every project.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Given that this is under the city-run Michelama, what can HPD do to expedite those permits to make sure that work is done in a more timely fashion than we typically see in our offices?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So sometimes, and I'm not 100% familiar with this project, but in some circumstances, and a lot of circumstances, we require permits to be pulled prior to closing.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I don't know exactly which permits need to be pulled for this project, but we make every effort with all of our affordable housing projects to ensure we're communicating with DOB and that our projects are getting permits pulled quickly.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We see, and I'm glad Amalgamated is here, we see a lot of costs.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: because of delays of things like permits or in their cases, DOB inspectors coming in.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And again, I understand it's not your agency, but you work in the same administration, you work in the same government.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And so you should be doing everything for our affordable housing to expedite those processes.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Otherwise,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: My office tries to do it, but we're a different branch of government.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You are in the same branch of government as these other agencies.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And that is a way to make sure the residents of our Mitchell Llamas and our limited equity co-ops can get the housing they deserve and save money, in their case, by not having those sheds up for...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Months and months on end.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And that was the case at Tracy Towers, by the way, in the late 2010s, when they had their sheds up for years.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It was years, and it wasn't until the controller called, made a call in, and it was gone.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: These are just bureaucratic hurdles that cost residents money.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Part of the speed report that was released tries to get to the root of some of those problems, is to kind of get out of our own way to make sure we can do development much more quickly and much more transparently.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So speed was a big attempt at moving that forward.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz clarifies that the $40M HPD put into Tracy Towers is a loan, not a grant, and expresses concern that residents were told it was an investment. Phillips confirms it's a loan with no principal/interest payments due, deferring and accruing to the end of the term.
chapters.title: Council Member Dinowitz on Tracy Towers $40M HPD loan as deferred debt

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I want to ask about the $36 million of Tracy.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It is for elevator replacements.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There were security.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Elements in there, I believe, like the gate for the parking lot.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Was cameras part of that, the scope of work as well?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Security system.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: From 2016?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The $36 million recent one, yes.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Okay, so yeah, so we, HPD actually put in, I believe, around $40 million in city capital.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And the scope is from broadly garage work, some plumbing repairs.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Oh, sorry, that's phase two.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Elevators, facade, plaza repairs, security upgrades, playground improvements.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay, I just want to be clear.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Tell me if I'm off base, but when you say HPD put in $40 million, what you mean is that the city council, through its city of yes negotiations, allocated more funding and secured more funding

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: for city-run michelamas, and Assemblyman Zaccaro secured $10 million, and the $40 million is actually a loan, correct?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: HPD lent $40 million in city capital like we would any other affordable housing project.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So $40 million of city capital was lent to the project with deferred, sorry, no principal or interest payments due, fully deferring and accruing to the end of the loan term.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Right, but it is a, I just want to be clear, it's a loan.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So this isn't the city investing money to improve.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It is the city investing a loan that at some point has to be paid back.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I mean, sure.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Yeah, okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Loans have to be paid back.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I just want to make sure we're clear because when we fight for this funding in the city government or when our colleagues in government secure this funding and we celebrate,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Because we're going to see improvements to the building.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And to find out later that this is just a loan, it feels a little different than it should.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It's a loan where no principal and interest payments are due.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Until it's due.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Until the balloon is due at the end of 30 years.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And then at 30 years, well, you know, I guess their children will be dealing with it.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The, yeah.

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks what mechanisms HPD has to hold contractors accountable after work is completed, citing Tracy Towers' history of repairs that didn't last. Phillips describes monthly monitoring during construction and warranty periods. Alika notes HPD teams remain available post-construction.
chapters.title: Council Member Dinowitz on warranty accountability for completed capital work

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I have one more question.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The same concerns about the elevators, the same concerns about the repairs, the loans that were taken out in 2016 remain the same today.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The residents at Tracy Towers say, well, 10 years ago, they told us they were fixing the elevators and they're still broken.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There isn't a day where all six elevators in either building are working.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I think last we checked, you were there 40 times this year.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The fire department had to come for the elevators.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Those are not fixed elevators.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And so for the residents of Tracy Towers, what guarantees do they have that HPD is conducting the oversight to ensure that the money that our tax dollars are being invested in, that we are investing in our affordable housing with these loans?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There's oversight over that and the quality of the work and that if we replace all the elevators, that in two years, they're not all going to break down again.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That if they replace the roof, it's not going to start leaking in two years.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: What mechanisms do you have to hold accountable the people who do the work?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So like I mentioned before, we have monthly construction requisitions where the contractors

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: is only paid based on work that has been completed.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So there's someone from HPD because we have city capital.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: There's someone from HDC because they're the first mortgage lender.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Can I just clarify my question?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I'm not sure you understood it.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Maybe I didn't say it right.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: No, I think I got it.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I think what we're trying, what we need to parse here is what is the actual scope of work?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Is it a repair or is it a complete replacement?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Complete replacement of elevators, which I think was in the scope of work for this particular building.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: But not in 2016.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Not in 2016.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: So let's say 2026, we're looking forward.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Scope of work, brand new elevators, right?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You have, let's just go with that one project.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: You requisition, the work's being done, great.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: After the work is completed and after the contractor has left,

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Is HPD going to stay and monitor it to make sure it is continuing to function?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Are they going to stay to hold the contractor accountable if it breaks down, if in five years they still don't have working elevators?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Or is HPD's only job to make sure that the work is being done on a month-by-month basis?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Our main goal is to make sure that the work is done correctly at the time it's being done, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we're ensuring that the requisitions that are going through are being processed and paid correctly and the work is done as it should.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We can have oversight and we can work with the owner to make sure that they are holding the contractor's feet to the fire.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: There is also typically a warranty period, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So if after...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: A year, there are problems with the elevator.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It's incumbent upon the owner to ensure that they are going back to the manufacturer or the contractor and holding them to the warranty standards.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And that's standard across all affordable housing projects.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: co-ops or rentals.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And just a little bit about housing supervision, the portfolio analysts will still be there.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: The constituent services coordinators will still be there.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: The technical services team will still be there.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: The engineers that work at HPD will still be there.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: So whenever there's an issue, we will still be able to call on folks within across the agency to come out.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: And so it doesn't end when a project ends.

utterances.speaker: Alika Amprey-Samuel
utterances.text: We're still there.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Yeah, it sounds like there's room to grow in this regard, given the experience.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Tracy, I see amalgamated nodding their heads at all of our other Mitchell alums.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It seems like there's room to grow.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I don't know if the one-year warranty was a...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Hypothetical or like was that what the warranty typically is one year?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It's a hypothetical.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Every contractor is responsible for completing the work to the owner.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Typically things that are installed have a warranty period.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Typically roofs have a warranty period, elevators, different mechanical equipment.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It varies based on what's installed.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It sounds like there's room to grow in this area and work to be done together on this because the work done 10 years ago had a warranty and it wasn't fixed.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And we're concerned the work going forward at any of these Mitchellamas around the

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: city, that we make sure as a city we are holding accountable these contractors who are ostensibly doing the work, and then it falls on the tenants to pay for more repairs after the work's done.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And we know things go slowly in government, that a year, two years is going to go by before there's any oversight.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: We want to work together to make sure we're holding those accountable.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: To keep costs down, to use all the tools in our toolbox.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And I just, again, I want to thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: There's lots of work to be done on this to keep these rents down, to maintain that promise of affordability that was promised to us from this administration.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I stand ready to work with all of you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: The tenants stand ready to work with you, but they deserve dignity and affordability in their affordable housing developments.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We don't clap in hearings.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We only, we do the silent approval gesture, but.

chapters.summary: Sanchez relays questions from a constituent via Council Member Banks about vacant units past 90-day windows and whether HPD audits after phase one of a three-phase increase. Phillips says HPD monitors whether future increases are needed and wants vacant units brought up to code.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez on vacant units and phased increase reviews

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: All right, thank you so much, Council Member Dinowitz.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I just want to add a note on his line of questioning around the scopes of work with, you know, finance through HPD funds, that this is something that we hear.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And I know I still think about you sometimes as Council Member, and Pre-Samuel, like we hear this all the time about HPD finance work, you know, something that's financed five years ago is now starting to have leaks.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Like, you know, there was all of those, there were all those articles about the South Bronx buildings, the luxury buildings that received some HPD financing.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And so it's just, it's an area that we want to make sure we have clarity on and we know how to follow up with you when there are problems.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: I have a question from...

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Ms.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Pam, who I don't know, who came to us from Councilmember Banks, so you're getting a question from Ms.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Pam via Councilmember Banks via me, which is, what steps have the supervising agencies taken internally to make sure owners are not leaving apartments empty past the 90-day window?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: The loss of rental income has a devastating impact on the financial health of these developments.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: A recent Michelama meeting confirms this is a widespread problem for a number of Michelama rental and co-op developments.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And the second question is, will the agencies implement a plan to conduct a needs assessment or audit after year one of a three-phase rent-carrying charge?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: increase to make sure that the second and third phase increases are necessary and then provide a detailed report of their findings to the residents of those individual developments.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So the review of the future rent increases, I mean, we're looking at the income and expense statements in the audits and can clearly see whether or not the increase is needed or not.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: In terms of the vacant units, you know, we absolutely do not want vacant units in Mitchell-Lamas, right?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We are...

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: In the midst of an affordable housing crisis, we are trying to build our way out of it.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: That is not the only strategy.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We do want to ensure that any unit that is vacant is brought up to code and habitable and safe for a new family to occupy.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And when we're having trouble balancing the budgets, when we're having trouble just meeting expenses, it leaves very little left over to do those repairs in individual apartments.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: And unfortunately, sometimes we have to wait for the larger capital improvements.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: To make those

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: upgrades.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Right, got it.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And so just with respect to the second question of, you know, you said you can see when,

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: what increases are needed, is there any circumstances in which a 10% increase this year means we don't need a 5% or whatever has been requested the following year?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: I mean, it's project by project.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: We have deferred in the past.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It is project by project.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: It is taking a critical look.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: at the existing expenses and projecting future expenses.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: If we can pull any other levers on the expenses to lower certain expenses, then that will absolutely mitigate any potential increase.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: So we keep a close eye on those things as the phased increases happen.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks how many Mitchell-Lamas have been lost from the program. Phillips says 65 city-supervised developments have exited. Sanchez confirms that's when the loan balloon comes due—when a Mitchell-Lama wants to exit the program.
chapters.title: Chair Sanchez on Mitchell-Lama program exits and loan balloons

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And just another question, just piggybacking off Councilmember Dinowitz and the ballooning loans.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: You know, yes, they are technically loans, but they are actually kind of grants so long as you stay in the program.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And so my last question is about who has stayed in the program.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So 269 Michilamas have been developed since 1955 statewide.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Can you tell us how much we have lost, how many we have lost?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: City super loans?

utterances.speaker: Adam Phillips
utterances.text: Okay, 65 city super loans.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: We've lost 65.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So these are folks, Councilmember Dinowitz, to your point, that pay back their loans, right?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And that's the way that you exit the program, is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: So that's when the loan comes due is when a Michilama wants to exit the program.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Chairs Sanchez and Lee thank HPD for their testimony and encourage agency staff to stay for public testimony. Lee reviews public testimony rules including two-minute limits and written submission procedures.
chapters.title: Chairs thank HPD and transition to public testimony

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Well, with that, I want to thank you so much for your testimony today, for all of these hours of hanging out with us.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: But we really do look forward to that roundtable and, you know, to make sure that Mitchell Lamas and our affordable developments are, you know, they remain front and center.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I say this to all the agencies, but if there are folks that can stay and listen to the public testimony, I think that would be great to hear from some of the residents firsthand.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It would be wonderful.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you so much for all of your time today.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And with that, we are going to move into public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so as they transition, I'm now opening the floor to public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So before we begin, I remind members of the public that this is a formal government proceeding and that decorum shall be observed at all times.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And as such, members of the public shall remain silent at all times.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The witness table is reserved for people who wish to testify.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No video recording.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: or photography is allowed from the witness table.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Further,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Further, members of the public may not present audio or video recordings as testimony, but may submit transcripts of such recordings to the Sergeant at Arms for inclusion in the hearing record.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If you wish to speak at today's hearing, please fill out an appearance card with the Sergeant at Arms and wait for your name to be called.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Once you have been recognized, you will have two minutes to speak on today's hearing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If you have a written statement or additional written testimony you wish to submit for the record, please provide a copy of that testimony to the sergeant at arms.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And you may also email written testimony to testimony at council.nyc.gov within 72 hours of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Audio and video recordings will not be accepted.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I just want to emphasize that our council staff literally read every single word of your testimonies, and we take that back into consideration and for feedback.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I just really encourage you all, if you have other neighbors, friends that live in the residence that you really want to have to submit testimony, please make sure they do that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: They have up to 72 hours from today.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: To submit it to the email address.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If you need it again, I can give it to you later.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so when you hear your name, please come up to the witness panel.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, you're good.

chapters.summary: Hill describes chronic elevator breakdowns, flooding from 52-year-old plumbing, substandard repairs, lack of security cameras, and 60% of residents not qualifying for subsidies. She criticizes HPD for approving work that doesn't last and demanding money without delivering service.
chapters.title: Jean Hill, Tracy Towers Tenant Organization President, testifies on building conditions

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so our first panel, we have Jean Hill, Ed Yeager, and Sally Stroman.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I apologize if I'm mispronouncing your names.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So if you could please come to the front table, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: Are you ready?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So you can start in whichever order.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think you just have to push the button.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: Okay, my name is Jean Hill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: I'm the president of the Tracy Towers Tenant Organization.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: I'm here representing Tracy Towers with a lot of my Tracy Towers residents here with me.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: I have been there since 1975.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: I've listened to everything that's going on today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: I found some of it extremely interesting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: I'll just give you a brief history of what's going on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: So we got $10 million two years ago from the assembly office to do work on the elevators and pointing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: None of that work has ever gotten started.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: I don't know why they're holding on to this money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: The elevators are constantly breaking down.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: People are constantly getting stuck in the elevators.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: The building leaks like a sieve because the plumbing is 52 years old.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: If they even touch it to go repair stuff, it breaks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: It floods the building.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: We've had apartments that have been totally flooded in certain lines.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: One of the apartments, they had to run the hose from their outside hallway into their apartment because the whole apartment was flooded and then back out to the terrace.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: Okay?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: The people couldn't even close their door.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: So they had to go three days with it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: their door wide open while this water has been sucked out of the building.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: We know that the leaks are one of the main problems.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: We know the elevators are a main problem.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: The fire department should have an apartment in the building.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: They're so constantly getting people out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: The 4th of July, the heat was on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: It was 96 degrees outside, and they had the heat going on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: This is just one of the many things that goes on in there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: We know all of these things have been happening.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: We have pleaded, begged, conjoled.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: And the other thing that pisses us off the most, and excuse me for that word,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: They come in and they do this work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: The work is substandard.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: They came in in 2012.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: They put in new cabinets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: The cabinets all falling apart.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: They put in new windows.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: We were still using fuses, and they came in and put in breaker switches in 2012.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: That's how long it took us to get.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: Breaker switches in the apartments.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: So most of the work that they do there is substandard.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: They say they come back and they check.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: I have never seen anybody in there checking, coming back to say, is this work completely done correctly?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: Does the work stand up?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: They did the roof some years ago.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: They had a 20-year warranty on the roof.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: The roof warranty has not even expired, and they're back asking for money to do the roof again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: We don't have cameras around the part of the building, so anybody can wander in and out of there at will.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: You know, they talk about they need all...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: All of this extra money for security.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: We know the security is substandard.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: Anybody could walk in.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: You could walk in there right now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: Nobody would stop you and ask you, what are you doing here?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: You're going to see somebody.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: We have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: A system of supposed to be you can ring the bell, but that doesn't work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: That hasn't worked since they put that in.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: So everything that they put in is substandard work and it does not last.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: But yet you keep coming back to the tenants asking for all of this money and we're not getting service for the money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: And then you have all of these people who don't fall under any subsidy program.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: At least 60% of the residents are not going to fall under subsidy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: And what are they supposed to do?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: Nobody's going to increase their salary because Tracy Towers wants more money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: Okay, so where they're supposed to go find it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: I have in the past had to work two and three jobs to pay the money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: So I know what that's like and raise a family at the same time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: And you have people in there with small children.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: So we have a lot of issues in there that people really seriously need to pay attention to.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: I know Julie Walpert for the last 40 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: I don't know how long she's been there.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: She says less than that, but I remember at least seeing her at least that long.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: I know most of them from HPD.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: It's not that we don't send in the 311 reports.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: It's not that we don't make the complaints.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: It's not that we don't film everything.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: But it's like I'm talking to myself and who's paying attention.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: So we are so glad to be able to come down here today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: I know some of the council members.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: I see them on occasion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: I'm also, you know, working the community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: So we really need somebody to really be paying attention to what HPD is supposed to be doing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: It's what they say they do and what they actually do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: And we're not getting the actual work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: We're just getting a fantasy land.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: And we tied it to fantasy land where we got to put good money out for it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Yeager advocates for including all limited equity housing in policy discussions, recommends a task force with council, HPD, DOB, and co-op boards to reduce city-mandated compliance costs, and highlights co-ops' community contributions including NORC programs.
chapters.title: Ed Yeager, Coordinating Council of Cooperatives, testifies on limited equity housing needs

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jean Hill
utterances.text: Yes, you can go ahead, sir.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: Ed Yeager, chair of the Coordinating Council of Cooperatives and Amalgamated Houses, as Eric knows.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: I thank the council and the committees for holding this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: It's an important topic.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: I'll start with two key points.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: The hearing specifies Mitchell-Lama.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: But it's important to include all limited equity housing, limited dividend, redevelopment, as well as Mitchell Lama.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: State supervised as well as city supervised.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: We're all New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: We're all part of this community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: Second, one of the financial burdens we have is compliance with city policies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: I'd like to recommend

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: a task force including the council, staff, HPD, Department of Buildings,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: and management and board of limited equity co-ops.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: We can find ways to reduce the city's burden without diminishing our safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: We know the difference between safety and overzealous rulemaking that simply adds to our cost.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: And doesn't reduce our burden.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: CCC are mostly large campus-style co-ops.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: We provide more than just housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: We provide community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: Our members created the first NARC programs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: Allowing seniors to age in place and saving the city money on costs that would otherwise accrue for health, etc.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: We all need financial assistance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: We're paying for years of deferred maintenance and years deferred

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: carry charge increases.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: These developments all have income restrictions and were designed for people who could meet the maintenance charges, but now we're facing

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: costs for rebuilding infrastructure, insurance skyrocketing,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: and the array of city mandates.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: We can't maintain the growing costs and maintain affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: So if we were

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: together we could reduce some of those costs and you have expertise we who actually live there have expertise to bring to the table thank you thank you

chapters.summary: Stroman raises concerns about the bidding process favoring the same vendors, excessive change orders increasing costs, and calls for a tenant committee to address issues across co-ops citywide.
chapters.title: Sally Stroman testifies on bidding process and change order costs

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: I'd like to thank this council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: You are all spot on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: You left me a little bit to say, but I'm going to say what I'm going to say anyway.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: First of all,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: The housing rate in New York City as of 2024 was 1.4%.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: Obviously, there need more housing to be built.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: That's a big question and a big job to do.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: My second question is,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: Mitchell-Lamba programs are insufficient.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: Oversight, maintenance, backlog, financial mismanagement, and instability.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: The bidding process needs to be looked into as the same vendors work on developments over and over throughout the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: Secondly, when contracts are signed, why are there so many change orders?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: Every time there's a change order,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: For instance, if we sign a contract for plumbing, I'm just making up a number, let's say for a million dollars.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: Why is there a change order that they missed something, they didn't look at something, they found something?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: That's a cost to the co-op.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: My co-op is not in a particularly unique situation, but it's throughout the city of New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: And I'm begging the council to come up with a solution and work with all co-ops to have maybe a tenant committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: Where we can all sit down and address our issues to you because we're the ones that's living in the conditions that I hear many of my

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: sister co-ops, regardless if it's city or state.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sally Stroman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do any of the council members have questions?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I just want to thank you so much for coming down.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know it's a long way.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think I saw you at the park the other day at Ford Independence Park, as Eric likes to joke.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm always in his district.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know, I know.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then Ms.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Jean Hill is yours and not mine.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That's okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But thank you for coming.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I hope that you know that we're going to continue to follow up on this.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And as you heard, there's going to be this roundtable that we're going to put together.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we'll let you know about that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And thank you, HPD, for still being here.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Hearing everything they have to say.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, so do I.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Nice.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Only one?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so next we have Eric Furman.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If you could come up.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You have a solo panel.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You by yourself.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: St.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ed Yeager
utterances.text: James Park, of course.

chapters.summary: Foreman describes HIT's $2.2B investment in NYC affordable housing since 9-11, including $569M in Mitchell-Lamas, applauds city capital commitments, and calls for thinking big about preservation with union jobs.
chapters.title: Eric Foreman, AFL-CIO Housing Investment Trust, testifies on preservation financing

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: Should I begin?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: Well, first, I'd like to thank Chair Lee and Chair Sanchez and all members of the City Council present for holding this important forum, and also thank the representatives of HPD and other city officials for being here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: It's a crucial meeting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: But most of all, I'd like to thank the many tenants in the audience for speaking up.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: It's difficult for working people to take time out of their day to engage in democracy, but it's critical.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: So myself, my name is Eric Foreman.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: I'm Director of Housing Policy for the AFL-CIO Housing Investment Trust.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: We are a social impact fund that invests union pension dollars in union-built affordable housing, new construction and preservation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: HIT has invested since 9-11 when HIT launched an initiative to help rebuild the city's economy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: We have invested $2.2 billion in affordable housing development projects in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: Of that, $569 million has been invested in Mitchell Llamas and affordable cooperative housing organized under other articles.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: We want to applaud the city for committing capital resources to the cooperatives and the rental Mitchell Llamas.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: We work very closely with the cooperatives that we have helped finance and refinance in order to meet their needs to keep the co-ops in a state of good repair while maintaining affordability.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: We also are a voice in Washington, D.C.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: We're a national fund for augmenting the federal resources that our city is very dependent on for maintaining our affordable housing stock.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: I think many great points have been made here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: I've offered more detail in the two-page written testimony that I submitted.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: But in the last 20 seconds, maybe I just want to leave you with a couple points.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: One is that from what we've seen, public investment is needed probably beyond the scale that has been committed in the capital budget to help stabilize the cooperatives.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: That said, private capital investment from sources such as HIT is a great resource.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: This is a moment where we all need to think big about how to preserve this resource.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: In the process, we should make sure that we create good union jobs for tenants who are also workers all across the city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Eric Foreman
utterances.text: Thanks.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Eric.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, next we have Ryan Schallenberger, Janice Brody, and Susan Peters.

chapters.summary: Brody describes Clayton Apartments' $15,000/month deficit, $700K insurance costs, and $25M+ in needed repairs, arguing Mitchell-Lamas should be treated as infrastructure receiving capital grants rather than loans that add to debt.
chapters.title: Janice Brody, Clayton Apartments board member, testifies on deficits and need for grants not loans

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: You want to go first?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: Because the two of us are together.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: I'm a board member for Clayton Apartments, located in Harlem, Central Harlem, on West 135th Street.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: Currently, our co-op, we're running at a deficit, at least $15,000 a month.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: We do have an IPNA.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: In process, but it looks like we may need between $25 million or more to get our building up to standard.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: We do have something in the process we need to get.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: I mean, we had a grant for like $150,000, but it turned out we can't get that unless we flip over another loan first.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: So we got to get into debt further just to get a six figure that we want to use for our double breaks for the elevator.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: We have to get that done by January 27.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: So we're constantly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: operating in a deficit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: And when I listened here earlier to, we're talking about in 27 and 28, we're going to have all this money and capital funding.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: And you just kind of broke it down by saying, it's going to be a loan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: It's not going to be an investment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: We need investment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: I mean, I think you need to look at the Mitchell Llamas as being part of the city infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: We should be treated as, we shouldn't be treated as, I don't know, debt.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: We're being treated as debt.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: And basically right now, as far as, I mean, we went through a lot of numbers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: I'm just going to throw a couple at you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: Our insurance, $700,000 a year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: We do have a lot of seniors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: The scree, that's not even a Band-Aid.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: I don't know what it's going to cover.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: But when we look at where we're located, there's going to be one big project that's going to have over 1,000 apartments.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: It's called 1-145 on 145th Street in Lenox.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: You're giving them big tax breaks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: There's going to be another building, three buildings across the street from us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: Big tax breaks, but we got to go into debt.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: So what we're asking for, we're not asking for a loan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: We're asking you to come in, say it's capital, and help repair our building.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: We don't need another loan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: We don't need a balloon coming.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: And we got balloons already.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: My building is 62 years old.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: So we are ready.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: We've been turning over and turning over.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: And over till we can't turn over no more.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: We need help, and I'm trying to make sure I'm covering everything because I didn't know I had two minutes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: So, yeah, Ms.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: Sanchez earlier mentioned the breaks that the renters are getting, the tax breaks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: The co-ops are not getting any breaks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: And like I said, we got to repair the elevator by 2027.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: All these local laws, Fairfield Avenue co-ops, they said they was going to put together a petition, and they sent one to you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: We're going to get a petition going to these local laws.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: Y'all should be covering them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: Our rent can only cover operating.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: We can't cover the local laws.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: It's just a little bit too much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: And I'm trying to go through all the figures of everything that we owe.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: But we need to be treated like we're infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: And when you say capital, say capital.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: If it's a loan, then be true and say it's a loan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: Because I was like, oh, yeah, we're getting all this money, 27, 28.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: And then you broke it down, and it's really a loan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: We are ready in debt.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: So this is another board member.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: So I'm going to stop now and let him.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: Did I cover everything?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Janice Brody
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Schallenberg details $250K+ spent on Legionella remediation with no reimbursement, $697K annual insurance, poor approved management companies including one indicted for theft, and lack of revenue-generating assets compared to other Mitchell-Lamas.
chapters.title: Ryan Schallenberg, Clayton Apartments VP, testifies on Legionella costs and management issues

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Hey everybody, my name is Ryan Schallenberg.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: I'm the vice president of the board.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: I'm going to try to say these things as quickly as possible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: So you mentioned

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: The loan conversion and how much of that, how much of the increases go towards the loan conversion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: I can tell you that HPD approved for us for this year, 15% increase, and that's only to cover the loan conversion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: And I'll be honest with you, that's nowhere, it's a drop in the bucket to get us up to speed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: So it's,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: You know, this idea that we're never going to pay the loans.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: We got approved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: I know Yusuf isn't here today, but Yusuf Salam, he got us $150,000 for 2026 fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: We can't even get that until we pay to convert the loan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: So it's essentially we've got nothing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Esplanade Gardens got a million dollars last year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: I don't know if they had to do the same thing, but they got that money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: We got nothing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: And, you know, Legionella is in the news right now.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: I know everyone's, that's on everybody's, I got you, don't worry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: I know that's on everybody's mind.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: We had Legionella in our building for three years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: When it happened, we were told by the Department of Health that we had to immediately take care of it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: We got zero money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: We had to pay over $250,000 to date.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: We're paying $6,000 to $8,000 a month to run a monochloramine system that we don't even need anymore.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: And we've had now three months of testing and been Legionella free.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Now, why do we have that problem?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Because we got 62-year-old pipes and the Legionella keeps coming back.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: So if we don't get the money to replace the pipes, it's going to keep coming again and again.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: And they're being forced to pay for this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Now, all these buildings in East Harlem right now are being cleaned out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: They're getting the money ahead of time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: We've been told we can't get that money back now that we paid for it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: And it's put us in a huge hole, quite frankly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: And that's the least of our problems.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: $25 to $30 million to update the pipes, the windows, the other local laws.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: I mean, I just don't know where to begin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Our insurance costs $697,000 a year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: I appreciate that that was addressed before, that you guys are going to do something about that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: But this is a systemic problem for Mitchell Lama.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: It's not just us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: And, okay, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: I think that's pretty much it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: But we appreciate everything you guys are trying to do and will continue to do for us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And if you do think of something after today, make sure to send it in because we will review it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm sorry, say that again?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No, I was saying if you do remember something after today, because there may have been some things that you forgot to mention, definitely submit that to us.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Got plenty to send you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: I appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, go ahead.

chapters.summary: Peters, a former Wall Street bank data professional, discusses how private banks create money through lending and argues the government should study this system's impact on city finances and affordability.
chapters.title: Susan Peters testifies on monetary policy and banking system

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: Can you hear me?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: My name is Susan Peters, and I am a resident of the Upper West Side of Manhattan and a member of the American Monetary Institute, a 30-year-old 501c3, working for monetary education and reform.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: I am here sharing with you an important piece of information relevant to your responsibility for the finances of our city and its effects on our citizens, such as this rising unaffordability crisis.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: First, my background.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: I had a 37-year career as a data processing professional.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: The last 17 years were spent working for a Wall Street bank.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: I was responsible for reporting the daily activity of the corporate checking account system.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: Each night of the week, the system delivered a balance sheet, which recorded each night.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: $2 trillion moving daily through the 40,000 accounts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: As the person responsible for the accounting application, I discovered to my surprise the special privilege given to private banks but not taught in our schools.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: Quote, every time a bank makes a loan, all it is doing is simply making a bookkeeping entry in the borrower's account for the full amount of the loan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: End quote.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: This bookkeeping entry is new money and increases the national money supply.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: Thus, the money supply is composed of debt to private banks and generates interest to those banks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: It's not taught in our schools or discussed in our public forums.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: But is significant to our understanding of our city's finances.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: That's why I'm here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: Our government needs to study this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: Please contact me with any questions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: I can meet with any interested party and share resources for further understanding.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: And in here, I have some of those resources from the bank.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: Of England and from a credit manager from a Federal Reserve Bank supporting what I've said.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Susan Peters
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think Councilmember Dynowitz, you had a question?

chapters.summary: Dinowitz asks Schallenberg about insurance costs per unit. Schallenberg calculates $4,300/unit. Dinowitz compares this to Tracy Towers' $4,600/unit and notes ANHD's estimate of $1,770/unit for affordable housing, calling for HPD to negotiate better rates.
chapters.title: Council Member Dinowitz questions Clayton Apartments on insurance costs per unit

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Yeah, Mr.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Schallenberger, you mentioned insurance costs.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: That was, I guess over the years, HPD has shared different reasons why rent has increased.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Insurance costs are one of them.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: They're going up everywhere.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Do you know how much you pay per unit?

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: For insurance?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: If anybody's good at math, you can divide 697,000 by 162, and that'll give it to you.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I can't do this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Janice is going to do it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Can I take this opportunity to actually...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: I just want to...

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Oh, please.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: $4,300 per unit for insurance.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: At Tracy Towers, it is also similar.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: It's $4,600 per unit for insurance.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: $4,600 per unit, roughly, for insurance.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: A&HD says that for affordable units, throughout the city, it's roughly $1,770 per unit for affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And the Rent Guidelines Board's documents that for rent-stabilized units, it's less than $2,000 a unit for insurance.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: Insurance costs are going up.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: But there is a real, seems like a real critical discrepancy between the two.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: costs Michilama residents are paying for insurance and what other types of affordable housing developments and apartments are paying for insurance.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And this is one of the areas I believe HPD needs to use their, I guess, negotiating power representing 46,000 units to get better rates because they are not in line with other types of affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Eric Dinowitz
utterances.text: And you all have to pay the price for those inflated insurance costs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: I appreciate you addressing that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Could I have 10 seconds?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Two other quick things?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: So the one other thing that I wanted to get to was the management companies, which people have talked about a little bit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: There's a list of about 30 that are approved.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Most of them are terrible.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: I'm just here to tell you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: We've looked into all of them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: We've talked to boards from different co-ops.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: They're very bad.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: I feel like it would be a huge benefit, and we've talked to some private companies who are willing to go through all the hoops to get approved by HPD, but opening that up is going to be helpful because...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: I'll be honest with you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: Like we're trying.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: We've had management steal from us.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: We've had one of our managers is literally indicted by a DA.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: I don't know if she's in prison now, but like that happened.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: And like we've had shareholders that have had their their monthly maintenance checks literally stolen multiple times.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
utterances.text: And these are the companies we're forced to work with by HPD because they only approve a certain amount.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Ryan Schallenberg
